turns.content: I keep getting a RecursionError in my tree traversal. Can you help me figure out why?

Yes. To narrow it down: (1) what kind of tree structure is it (binary tree, n-ary, general graph masquerading as a tree)? (2) are there parent pointers or cross-links? (3) can you share the traversal code and a small example of the data structure that triggers the error?
It’s supposed to be a tree but I built it from an adjacency list, so maybe it’s a graph. Here’s the simplified code:

class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val self.children = [] def build_nodes(adj): nodes = {k: Node(k) for k in adj} for k, kids in adj.items(): nodes[k].children = [nodes[c] for c in kids] return nodes def dfs(node, visited=set()): visited.add(node.val) out = [node.val] for child in node.children: if child.val not in visited: out.extend(dfs(child, visited)) return out adj = { "A": ["B", "C"], "B": ["D"], "C": ["E"], "D": ["A"],  # oops cycle "E": [] } nodes = build_nodes(adj) print(dfs(nodes["A"]))
I expected it to stop because of visited, but I still get RecursionError sometimes in other cases, and also weird behavior between runs.

Two separate issues jump out: 1) The default argument visited=set() is a mutable default; it’s created once at function definition time and then reused across calls. That explains “weird behavior between runs” (more precisely: between separate calls to dfs within the same process). If you call dfs multiple times, visited retains old values. 2) With cycles, your visited logic should prevent revisiting, so in the shown code it should terminate. But if you sometimes see RecursionError “in other cases”, it can happen if: - Node values are not unique (visited by val fails to prevent revisiting the same Node object), or - The graph has multiple nodes with same val string, or - You’re building nodes in a way that creates distinct Node objects for the same key, so visited by val doesn’t match object identity issues, or - You have a cycle but visited isn’t being shared correctly due to that default argument being reused in unexpected ways, or you’re calling dfs with a fresh visited in some branches. Fix the default argument first: def dfs(node, visited=None): if visited is None: visited = set() visited.add(node)  # or node.val if unique ... Also: consider marking visited by node object identity, not by value: visited.add(node) if child not in visited: ... Can you show one of the “other cases” where it recurses forever?
I think values are unique, but I do call dfs multiple times in tests. Here’s another snippet. I simplified but it still happens:
def collect_paths(node, path=[], paths=[]): path.append(node.val) if not node.children: paths.append(list(path)) else: for ch in node.children: collect_paths(ch, path, paths) path.pop() return paths
Sometimes this goes crazy or produces duplicated paths. I know it’s a different function but similar pattern. Is that related?

Yes, it’s the same mutable-default pitfall, twice: both path=[] and paths=[] are shared across calls. That causes duplication and cross-test contamination. It can also create surprising recursion depth if paths or path are not reset as expected, but typically it manifests as “results accumulate”. Rewrite: def collect_paths(node, path=None, paths=None): if path is None: path = [] if paths is None: paths = [] path.append(node.val) if not node.children: paths.append(path.copy()) else: for ch in node.children: collect_paths(ch, path, paths) path.pop() return paths This addresses duplication across separate calls. Now, about “goes crazy”: if your structure isn’t a tree (has cycles), collect_paths will recurse infinitely because it doesn’t track visited at all. In a pure tree, it’s fine. In a graph, you need cycle detection (and decide what “paths” even means with cycles). Do you have any back-edges like D -> A in the data you pass to collect_paths?
Yes, sometimes. I thought I was building a tree but the input is messy. How do I adapt collect_paths to avoid cycles? I still want paths to leaves, but if there’s a cycle, I want to stop that branch.

Add a “currently in recursion stack” set (or visited-on-path) to prevent revisiting nodes already on the current path. For path enumeration, you usually don’t want a global visited that prevents reaching a node via a different route; you want to avoid cycles within a single path. Example using node identity: def collect_paths(node, path=None, paths=None, on_path=None): if path is None: path = [] if paths is None: paths = [] if on_path is None: on_path = set() if node in on_path: return paths  # cycle detected; stop this branch on_path.add(node) path.append(node.val) if not node.children: paths.append(path.copy()) else: for ch in node.children: collect_paths(ch, path, paths, on_path) path.pop() on_path.remove(node) return paths This ensures cycles don’t explode. If you want to treat a node with children but all children lead to cycles as a “dead end”, you could add logic to append a path when no progress is possible, but that’s a product decision.
That helps. But I still get RecursionError in dfs even after fixing visited default. It happens on deep trees (like linked list shape). Is increasing recursion limit the right fix?

For a very deep tree (depth > ~1000), Python’s recursion limit will trigger RecursionError even without cycles. Increasing the recursion limit is sometimes acceptable for controlled inputs, but it can risk crashing the interpreter if you set it too high (C stack overflow). Better: rewrite traversal iteratively with an explicit stack. Iterative DFS preorder: def dfs_iter(root): visited = set() out = [] stack = [root] while stack: node = stack.pop() if node in visited: continue visited.add(node) out.append(node.val) # push children in reverse if you care about order for ch in reversed(node.children): stack.append(ch) return out If it’s guaranteed to be a tree (no cycles), you can omit visited for speed; but given messy input, keep it. If you need postorder, you can do a two-stack trick or push (node, state) pairs.
I do need postorder for computing subtree sizes. My recursive version is:
def size(node): s = 1 for ch in node.children: s += size(ch) return s
But with deep trees it fails. Can you show iterative postorder?

Sure. Use an explicit stack with a “visited/expanded” flag: def size_iter(root): stack = [(root, 0)]  # (node, state) state 0=enter, 1=exit sizes = {}  # map node -> subtree size on_stack = set()  # optional cycle detection for messy graphs while stack: node, state = stack.pop() if state == 0: if node in on_stack: # cycle; decide policy. Here we skip reprocessing. continue on_stack.add(node) stack.append((node, 1)) for ch in node.children: stack.append((ch, 0)) else: # compute after children total = 1 for ch in node.children: total += sizes.get(ch, 0) sizes[node] = total on_stack.discard(node) return sizes.get(root, 0) If you have a true tree, drop on_stack and sizes.get fallback. In a graph with cycles, subtree size isn’t well-defined unless you define how to handle repeated nodes (DAG vs general graph). The above “skip cycles” will undercount in cyclic regions; it’s more of a protective measure. If your input can be a DAG (no cycles but shared subtrees), memoization is valuable; the iterative approach already stores sizes per node.

Good point. In my data there can be shared nodes (like two parents pointing to same child), so it’s a DAG sometimes. My recursive size double-counts shared nodes. I want each unique node counted once, reachable from root. How do I do that?

Then you’re not computing “subtree size” in the tree sense; you’re computing “number of distinct reachable nodes”. For a DAG or general graph, define size as count of unique nodes reachable from root. That’s a reachability problem; use DFS/BFS with a visited set: def reachable_count(root): visited = set() stack = [root] while stack: node = stack.pop() if node in visited: continue visited.add(node) stack.extend(node.children) return len(visited) If you need something like “sum of weights over unique nodes”, you can accumulate similarly. If you truly need a DP-like value on a DAG where shared nodes should not double count within a parent’s aggregation, that becomes “count of unique nodes in union of children’s reachable sets”, which is more complex (set union DP) and can be expensive. But for just “reachable from root”, the above is correct.
Understood. Another issue: I tried using node objects in a set and got “TypeError: unhashable type: 'Node'” in my real project. In the simplified code it worked, but in the real one it doesn’t. Why?

In Python, user-defined objects are hashable by default (their hash is based on identity) unless you define __eq__ without defining __hash__. Commonly, dataclasses with eq=True and frozen=False make instances unhashable. If your real Node is a @dataclass with eq=True, then it defines __eq__ and sets __hash__=None, making it unhashable. Fix options: - Use identity-based visited: visited_ids = set(); store id(node) - Or make the dataclass hashable (careful): @dataclass(eq=True, frozen=True) if immutable; or set unsafe_hash=True if you know what you’re doing. - Or define __hash__ to use object identity: __hash__ = object.__hash__ and keep __eq__ as identity or consistent. Example: from dataclasses import dataclass, field @dataclass class Node: val: str children: list = field(default_factory=list) __hash__ = object.__hash__ Then visited can store node objects. If you want equality by val, you can store val in visited, but only if val uniquely identifies a node globally.

My Node is a dataclass with val and children and also a parent pointer. I set eq=True so I can compare nodes by val. But then children and parent create recursion in equality maybe? Could that cause recursion errors too?

Yes. If dataclass-generated __eq__ compares all fields, and those fields include children lists and parent references, you can get deep recursion (or infinite recursion on cycles) just from doing node1 == node2, or from putting nodes into containers that use equality checks. Also, if you override __repr__ implicitly, printing nodes can recurse through children/parent. If you want nodes considered equal by val only, set dataclass fields to exclude from comparison: @dataclass(eq=True) class Node: val: str children: list = field(default_factory=list, compare=False, repr=False) parent: "Node|None" = field(default=None, compare=False, repr=False) Then __eq__ compares only val. But be cautious: if two different nodes can share the same val, you’ll conflate them. If you also want hashability consistent with equality, you can set frozen=True (if you can) or implement __hash__ based on val. But if val is mutable or not unique, that’s dangerous. Given parent pointers and potential cycles, I’d generally avoid structural equality and keep identity semantics for nodes, unless you have a strong reason.
That makes sense. In my logs, RecursionError sometimes happens when I print the node. I do print(node) inside traversal for debugging. Could __repr__ cause it?

Absolutely. If your Node’s __repr__ includes children, and children include parent, you can get an infinite representation loop. Dataclasses’ default repr includes all fields unless repr=False. Solution: - Set repr=False on recursive fields (children, parent). - Provide a custom __repr__ that prints only val (and maybe counts). Example: def __repr__(self): return f"Node(val={self.val!r}, children={len(self.children)})" Then debugging prints won’t traverse the whole structure.
Great. Now about my original dfs: I used visited=set() and fixed it. But I also have another function that uses recursion with caching and still hits recursion depth. Here:
from functools import lru_cache @lru_cache(None) def depth(node): if not node.children: return 1 return 1 + max(depth(ch) for ch in node.children)
It fails on deep chains. I thought caching would help.

Caching helps with repeated subproblems (DAG with shared subtrees), but it doesn’t reduce recursion depth on a single long chain. For a chain of length N, you still recurse N deep. If you need to handle deep chains, use iterative DP: - If it’s a tree/graph without cycles, you can compute depths via postorder topological processing. - For general graphs, “depth” may be undefined with cycles unless you define it. For a tree or DAG, you can compute height iteratively with a postorder stack (similar to size_iter) and store height per node. Example (tree/DAG, no cycles): def height_iter(root): stack = [(root, 0)] height = {} visiting = set() while stack: node, state = stack.pop() if state == 0: if node in visiting: # cycle detected; decide policy continue visiting.add(node) stack.append((node, 1)) for ch in node.children: stack.append((ch, 0)) else: if not node.children: height[node] = 1 else: height[node] = 1 + max(height.get(ch, 0) for ch in node.children) visiting.discard(node) return height.get(root, 0) This avoids recursion depth issues.
I also noticed lru_cache doesn’t accept my node because it’s unhashable (same dataclass issue). I worked around by caching by node.val, but then it breaks if two nodes share val. Any better approach?

If you want caching keyed by identity, cache by id(node) or by an assigned unique integer index. Example caching by id with a manual dict: def depth_id(root): cache = {} visiting = set() def helper(node): k = id(node) if k in cache: return cache[k] if k in visiting: # cycle return 0 visiting.add(k) if not node.children: d = 1 else: d = 1 + max(helper(ch) for ch in node.children) visiting.remove(k) cache[k] = d return d return helper(root) But this is still recursive; to avoid depth errors, use the iterative height_iter and store by node identity (node object as key if hashable, or id(node) if not). If you can make Node hashable by identity (__hash__ = object.__hash__), that’s the cleanest.
I’m going to adjust Node dataclass now. Here’s what I currently have:
@dataclass class Node: val: str children: list["Node"] = field(default_factory=list) parent: "Node|None" = None
What exact annotations/field options would you recommend?

For a graph-like node with parent pointers and children lists, I’d do: from dataclasses import dataclass, field from typing import Optional, List @dataclass(eq=False) class Node: val: str children: List["Node"] = field(default_factory=list, repr=False) parent: Optional["Node"] = field(default=None, repr=False) __hash__ = object.__hash__ def __repr__(self): return f"Node({self.val!r})" Key points: - eq=False: keep identity equality; avoids deep comparisons and unhashable issues. - __hash__ = object.__hash__: allows using Node in sets/dicts by identity. - repr=False on recursive fields + custom __repr__. If you truly need equality-by-val in some contexts, implement a separate comparator or compare node.val explicitly.

After changing eq=False, some tests that used node == other_node by val fail. But I can adjust them. Another bug: my build_nodes creates nodes only for keys in adj, but some children appear only in child lists and not as keys, so KeyError. How should I handle that?

Build nodes for all unique labels appearing as either parent or child: def build_nodes(adj): labels = set(adj.keys()) for kids in adj.values(): labels.update(kids) nodes = {k: Node(k) for k in labels} for k, kids in adj.items(): nodes[k].children = [nodes[c] for c in kids] for c in kids: nodes[c].parent = nodes[k]  # careful: multiple parents possible return nodes But note: setting parent like that assumes a tree (single parent). In a DAG/graph, a node can have multiple parents; then parent should be a list or you should omit it. If you need parent pointers only when structure is known to be a tree, validate input (detect multiple parents).
Right, multiple parents happen. I’ll remove parent pointer. Now: I want to detect cycles early when building. What’s a good way?

If you have adjacency list by labels, you can run a graph cycle detection (DFS coloring) before building Node objects, or after. For directed graph, classic 3-color DFS: WHITE=unvisited, GRAY=in stack, BLACK=done. def has_cycle(adj): WHITE, GRAY, BLACK = 0, 1, 2 color = {u: WHITE for u in adj} # ensure all nodes included for kids in adj.values(): for v in kids: color.setdefault(v, WHITE) def dfs(u): color[u] = GRAY for v in adj.get(u, []): if color[v] == GRAY: return True if color[v] == WHITE and dfs(v): return True color[u] = BLACK return False for u in color: if color[u] == WHITE and dfs(u): return True return False If you want to find the actual cycle path, keep parent pointers in the DFS stack. If cycles are allowed but you want to “break” them, you can remove back edges, but that’s a policy decision.
I want to actually report the cycle path for debugging. Can you show how to extract one cycle?

Yes. Track the current recursion stack list and an index map: def find_cycle(adj): WHITE, GRAY, BLACK = 0, 1, 2 color = {} stack = [] pos = {}  # node -> index in stack def visit(u): color[u] = GRAY pos[u] = len(stack) stack.append(u) for v in adj.get(u, []): if color.get(v, WHITE) == WHITE: cyc = visit(v) if cyc: return cyc elif color.get(v) == GRAY: i = pos[v] return stack[i:] + [v]  # close the loop stack.pop() pos.pop(u, None) color[u] = BLACK return None # ensure all nodes appear nodes = set(adj.keys()) for kids in adj.values(): nodes.update(kids) for u in nodes: if color.get(u, WHITE) == WHITE: cyc = visit(u) if cyc: return cyc return None This returns something like ["A","B","D","A"]. For very deep graphs, this recursive cycle finder can still hit recursion limit; you can implement iterative, but recursion is often okay for cycle detection if depth is manageable.
My graphs can be very deep, so recursion for cycle detection might also overflow. Is there an iterative cycle detection?

For directed graphs, iterative DFS with explicit stack and state can detect back edges. Sketch: def find_cycle_iter(adj): WHITE, GRAY, BLACK = 0, 1, 2 color = {} parent = {} iters = {} nodes = set(adj.keys()) for kids in adj.values(): nodes.update(kids) for start in nodes: if color.get(start, WHITE) != WHITE: continue stack = [start] parent[start] = None while stack: u = stack[-1] if color.get(u, WHITE) == WHITE: color[u] = GRAY iters[u] = iter(adj.get(u, [])) try: v = next(iters[u]) c = color.get(v, WHITE) if c == WHITE: parent[v] = u stack.append(v) elif c == GRAY: # reconstruct cycle u -> v back edge cycle = [v] x = u while x != v and x is not None: cycle.append(x) x = parent.get(x) cycle.append(v) cycle.reverse() return cycle except StopIteration: stack.pop() color[u] = BLACK iters.pop(u, None) return None This avoids recursion and returns one cycle.
Nice. Switching gears: In my traversal, I sometimes mutate node.children while iterating (filtering). Could that cause recursion errors?

It can cause logical errors (skipping elements, revisiting, unexpected structure), and in a recursive traversal it can indirectly create cycles or prevent visited from working as intended if you add edges. But it won’t directly raise RecursionError unless the mutation introduces a cycle or increases depth. If you need to prune children during traversal, iterate over a snapshot: for ch in list(node.children): ... Or perform pruning in a separate pass to keep traversal stable.
I think I accidentally appended parent as a child in one place, making cycles. How can I guard against that in code, besides visited?

Add invariants/validation: - When adding an edge parent->child, assert child is not ancestor of parent (cycle check). That’s expensive if done naively. - At least prevent self-loops: assert child is not parent. - Keep a debug-mode cycle detector that runs after building. - If you maintain parent pointers for a tree, ensure child.parent is None before assigning, and ensure you never add parent to children. In traversal functions, use both: - visited to avoid repeated work - on_path to detect cycles and report them with a path trace Example cycle-reporting traversal: def dfs_with_trace(root): out = [] visited = set() stack = [(root, 0)] path = [] on_path = set() while stack: node, state = stack.pop() if state == 0: if node in on_path: # found back edge; report cycle i = path.index(node) if node in path else None cycle = path[i:] + [node] if i is not None else [node] raise ValueError(f"Cycle detected: {[n.val for n in cycle]}") if node in visited: continue visited.add(node) on_path.add(node) path.append(node) out.append(node.val) stack.append((node, 1)) for ch in reversed(node.children): stack.append((ch, 0)) else: on_path.remove(node) path.pop() return out This is heavier but good for debugging.
I like that. One more subtlety: I used visited=set() default and fixed it, but I still see odd behavior in multithreaded runs. Could shared state be an issue?

If visited/path/paths are global variables or shared mutable objects across threads, yes. But if they’re local per call and you’re not using mutable defaults, they should be thread-safe per invocation. However, if Node objects themselves are shared and being mutated concurrently (children list modified), traversals can behave nondeterministically. If you run traversals in parallel, treat the graph as immutable during traversal or synchronize modifications. Also note: functools.lru_cache is not designed for mutation of cached inputs; and caching across threads can lead to surprising memory growth, but it’s thread-safe in CPython for basic use. If you can show the multithreaded pattern, I can point to specific hazards.

It’s like this: I build a graph once, then run multiple analyses in parallel threads, each traversing from different roots. No mutation intended, but sometimes one analysis prunes nodes (removes children) to simplify. I guess that’s the mutation.

Yes, that will race. If one thread prunes while another is traversing, the second thread can see partial modifications. Fix: - Don’t mutate shared graph; instead, create a filtered view (e.g., compute allowed nodes set and skip others during traversal). - Or deep-copy the relevant subgraph per analysis (can be expensive). - Or lock around mutations and ensure no traversals happen concurrently (reduces parallelism). A common approach: keep the original graph immutable; represent pruning as a predicate: def dfs_filtered(root, allow): visited = set() stack = [root] out = [] while stack: n = stack.pop() if n in visited or not allow(n): continue visited.add(n) out.append(n.val) for ch in reversed(n.children): stack.append(ch) return out Where allow(n) checks membership in a precomputed set.
That’s helpful. I think I can refactor to avoid mutation. Before I do, can you help me write a unit test that would catch mutable-default bugs like path=[]?

Sure. The simplest test calls the function twice and checks the second call’s output doesn’t include results from the first. Example with collect_paths: def test_collect_paths_no_state_leak(): # build tiny tree a = Node("A"); b = Node("B"); c = Node("C") a.children = [b, c] b.children = [] c.children = [] p1 = collect_paths(a) p2 = collect_paths(a) assert p1 == p2 assert p1 == [["A","B"], ["A","C"]] If the function uses default paths=[], the second call often returns duplicated paths (length 4). That catches it. Also test calling from different roots: def test_collect_paths_separate_roots(): x = Node("X"); y = Node("Y") x.children=[y]; y.children=[] a = Node("A"); a.children=[] assert collect_paths(x) == [["X","Y"]] assert collect_paths(a) == [["A"]] Mutable defaults can cause the second call to contain both.
Great. Can we also discuss performance? My graph can have millions of nodes, but edges are sparse. Python recursion is out; iterative is fine. What’s the most memory-efficient traversal?

For reachability/traversal, memory is dominated by visited. If you need to avoid revisiting, you must store something per visited node. Options: - Use integer node IDs and a bitset/bytearray if IDs are dense: visited = bytearray(n) - If IDs are sparse strings, map to integers first. - If you can tolerate revisits (tree), omit visited. - Use iterative stack; stack size is O(depth) for DFS; BFS queue is O(width) and can be larger. For millions of nodes, prefer: - Node represented by integer index - adjacency as list-of-lists (or array of arrays) - visited as bytearray - stack as Python list of ints Example: def dfs_ids(adj, start): n = len(adj) vis = bytearray(n) out = [] stack = [start] while stack: u = stack.pop() if vis[u]: continue vis[u] = 1 out.append(u) for v in adj[u]: if not vis[v]: stack.append(v) return out This is much more memory efficient than Node objects + sets.
Makes sense. My current representation is dict[str, list[str]]. I guess I should compress labels to ints. Any tips?

Build an index mapping: labels = set(adj.keys()) for kids in adj.values(): labels.update(kids) labels = sorted(labels)  # stable idx = {lab:i for i,lab in enumerate(labels)} adj_i = [[] for _ in range(len(labels))] for u, kids in adj.items(): ui = idx[u] adj_i[ui] = [idx[v] for v in kids] Now traverse using ints. If labels huge, avoid sorted; use list(labels) to keep arbitrary order. If you need reverse mapping: labels_list where labels_list[i] gives original label.
Awesome. I think I have enough. Final question: is there any safe way to keep recursion but avoid stack overflow, like trampolining?

You can implement trampolining or use generators to simulate recursion, but in Python it’s usually more complex and slower than just writing an explicit stack loop. Python doesn’t optimize tail calls. If you really want a “recursive style”, you can write functions that return “thunks” and loop until a value appears, but for tree traversal it becomes awkward because you have branching, not just tail recursion. So: for deep structures, explicit stack is the idiomatic and safest approach.
Thanks. I’ll refactor to iterative traversal, fix dataclass equality/repr, and add cycle detection. If I hit issues, I’ll come back.

Sounds good. If you share one representative adjacency sample and the exact metric you need (reachable count, postorder aggregation, path enumeration), I can help tailor an implementation that’s iterative, cycle-safe, and efficient for large sparse graphs.

I’m back. I refactored to iterative DFS and compressed labels to ints. But now I’m seeing something odd: my visited bytearray seems to “forget” nodes in some runs, and I get duplicates in the output list. I’m not using threads. Any idea what would cause that?

If you’re truly using a fresh bytearray per traversal, it shouldn’t “forget”. The usual causes are: 1) You’re reusing the same visited array across traversals without resetting it (or resetting incorrectly). With bytearray, it’s easy to accidentally keep a reference around. 2) You’re not marking visited at the right time. If you only mark visited when popping, duplicates can still appear in the stack and then you’ll append to output multiple times if you append before checking visited. 3) You’re mixing up node IDs and indices (e.g., using original labels or some other integer IDs that aren’t 0..n-1). If an ID exceeds length, you’d get IndexError, but if it’s negative you’ll index from the end and corrupt unrelated entries, leading to “forgetting” behavior. 4) You’re mutating adjacency while traversing (even unintentionally), causing the traversal to see different neighbors and revisit. Can you paste the exact iterative traversal function and how you call it twice in a row?
Sure. Here’s the function:
def dfs_ids(adj, start, visited=bytearray()): out = [] stack = [start] while stack: u = stack.pop() if visited[u]: continue out.append(u) visited[u] = 1 stack.extend(adj[u]) return out
And I call it like dfs_ids(adj_i, idx["A"]) multiple times in tests. I thought default args were fine for bytearray since it’s “small”.

That’s the same mutable-default bug again. visited=bytearray() is created once, reused forever, and it’s also initially length 0, which is another issue. Even if you did visited=bytearray(n), it would still be shared between calls and would “remember” prior runs (the opposite of forgetting), but because it’s length 0 you’ll actually hit IndexError unless something else is going on (maybe you omitted the snippet detail and you do allocate elsewhere). Fix it to allocate per call, with the correct size: def dfs_ids(adj, start, visited=None): n = len(adj) if visited is None: visited = bytearray(n) out = [] stack = [start] while stack: u = stack.pop() if visited[u]: continue visited[u] = 1 out.append(u) # Optional micro-opt: only push unvisited neighbors for v in adj[u]: if not visited[v]: stack.append(v) return out Also note I moved visited[u]=1 before out.append(u) isn’t required, but it avoids any future refactor where you append before the check.
Wow, I can’t believe I did that again. That explains tests contaminating each other. But what about “forgetting”? I saw duplicates within a single run sometimes.

Within a single run, duplicates in out can only happen if the “if visited[u]: continue” check is bypassed for some u that was previously marked visited. That points to either: - u is outside 0..n-1 (negative indices are the silent killer: visited[-1] is valid and refers to the last element). If your mapping ever produces -1 (e.g., dict.get with default -1), you’ll corrupt visited and get weird effects. - visited is being resized or replaced mid-run (unlikely unless you pass it in and something else mutates it). - You’re accidentally shadowing visited with a local variable somewhere else (less likely in this function). A strong defensive check while debugging: if not (0 <= u < n): raise ValueError(f"Bad node id {u}, n={n}") And similarly for each neighbor v. If you paste how you build idx and adj_i, I can point out where a -1 could slip in.
Here’s the compressor:

labels = set(adj.keys()) for kids in adj.values(): labels.update(kids) labels = list(labels) idx = {lab:i for i, lab in enumerate(labels)} adj_i = [[] for _ in range(len(labels))] for u, kids in adj.items(): ui = idx.get(u, -1) adj_i[ui] = [idx.get(v, -1) for v in kids]
I used get(..., -1) to avoid KeyError, thinking it’d be fine.

That -1 is exactly how you get “forgetting” and duplicates: -1 is a valid Python index (last element). So any missing label collapses onto the last node, merging edges and visited flags. That will create apparent revisits and weird traversal order. You shouldn’t silently map missing labels. Since you already built labels from both keys and kids, missing labels should not happen; if it does, it indicates a bug worth failing fast. Change to direct indexing and assert: for u, kids in adj.items(): ui = idx[u]  # KeyError if missing lst = [] for v in kids: if v not in idx: raise KeyError(f"Unknown label {v!r} referenced from {u!r}") lst.append(idx[v]) adj_i[ui] = lst Or, if you want to tolerate missing nodes by auto-adding them, do it explicitly before building adj_i (but you already do that with labels.update(kids)).

That makes total sense. I’ll remove the -1 fallback. Next issue: I implemented iterative cycle detection (the GRAY/BLACK version you showed) and it flags a cycle in data that I thought was a DAG. But I’m not sure if it’s a real cycle or if I messed up. How can I debug which edge caused it?

With the iterative cycle finder that returns a cycle path, the most direct debug is to print the labels along that returned cycle. If your cycle finder returns something like [v, ..., v], then each adjacent pair is an edge. The “causing” edge is the back edge from u to v where v was GRAY. If you want more explicit reporting, modify the cycle reconstruction to include the back-edge (u -> v) and maybe include original labels. If you have labels_list mapping int -> label: cycle = find_cycle_iter(adj_i) if cycle: print("Cycle:", " -> ".join(labels_list[i] for i in cycle)) Additionally, verify your adjacency itself isn’t wrong due to compression mistakes (the -1 issue could have created fake cycles). Fix compression first, then re-run cycle detection.

Right, I likely created fake cycles with -1. I’ll rerun after fixing. Another question: I need to compute “longest path to a leaf” in a DAG, but if there’s a cycle, I want to report it. What’s a robust approach without recursion?

For a directed graph, “longest path” is well-defined on a DAG. The robust workflow is: 1) Detect a cycle. If found, report it and stop (or handle per policy). 2) If no cycle, topologically sort. 3) Do DP in topo order (or reverse topo) to compute longest distance. Assuming edges go from parent -> child, and “leaf” means outdegree 0. Steps: - Kahn’s algorithm for topo sort is iterative and also detects cycles (if topo order doesn’t include all nodes, there’s a cycle). It won’t directly give you the cycle path, but you can combine it with a cycle finder for path reporting. - DP: process nodes in reverse topo so children are processed before parents: dp[u] = 1 if outdegree=0 else 1 + max(dp[v] for v in adj[u]) Pseudo-code: def topo_kahn(adj): n = len(adj) indeg = [0]*n for u in range(n): for v in adj[u]: indeg[v] += 1 q = [u for u in range(n) if indeg[u] == 0] order = [] # use list as stack or collections.deque as queue from collections import deque dq = deque(q) while dq: u = dq.popleft() order.append(u) for v in adj[u]: indeg[v] -= 1 if indeg[v] == 0: dq.append(v) return order  # if len(order)<n => cycle exists def longest_to_leaf_dag(adj, root): order = topo_kahn(adj) n = len(adj) if len(order) != n: cyc = find_cycle_iter(adj)  # for a path raise ValueError(f"Cycle detected: {cyc}") dp = [0]*n for u in reversed(order): if not adj[u]: dp[u] = 1 else: best = 0 for v in adj[u]: if dp[v] > best: best = dp[v] dp[u] = 1 + best return dp[root] This computes longest path length in nodes count. If you want edges count, drop the +1 base.
I also need to reconstruct the actual longest path, not just the length.

Store a “next pointer” choice during DP: next_node = [-1]*n for u in reversed(order): if not adj[u]: dp[u] = 1 next_node[u] = -1 else: best_v = adj[u][0] best = dp[best_v] for v in adj[u][1:]: if dp[v] > best: best = dp[v] best_v = v dp[u] = 1 + best next_node[u] = best_v Then reconstruct from root: path = [] u = root while u != -1: path.append(u) u = next_node[u] This gives one of possibly many longest paths.
Great. Now, about debugging: when something is off, I usually sprinkle print statements, but with millions of nodes that’s impossible. Any better debugging strategy for graph traversals?

A few practical strategies that scale: 1) Invariants + assertions on small samples - Before big runs, run validation on a small induced subgraph (e.g., first N nodes reachable from root) and verify properties: no -1 neighbors, neighbor IDs in range, optional acyclicity. 2) Deterministic “trace mode” on a tiny subset - Add a parameter max_steps or max_visits and stop after that, printing only a bounded trace. Example: stop after visiting 200 nodes and dump the current stack and path. 3) Logging counters not per-node logs - Track aggregate stats: visited count, max stack size, number of edges scanned, number of repeats skipped, etc. Print only at intervals (every 1e6 pops). 4) Save a reproducible failing slice - When you detect something bad (cycle, out-of-range neighbor), capture the local neighborhood: node u, its adjacency list, and maybe parents (reverse edges) for a small radius, and serialize that to JSON to reproduce in a unit test. 5) Use Python’s built-in debugger conditionally - Set a conditional breakpoint only when u == some suspicious node id, or when encountering invalid neighbor. Example counters: def dfs_debug(adj, start, report_every=1_000_000): n = len(adj) vis = bytearray(n) stack = [start] out = 0 pops = 0 max_stack = 1 while stack: u = stack.pop() pops += 1 if pops % report_every == 0: print("pops", pops, "visited", out, "stack", len(stack), "max_stack", max_stack) if not (0 <= u < n): raise ValueError(f"Bad u={u}") if vis[u]: continue vis[u] = 1 out += 1 for v in adj[u]: if not (0 <= v < n): raise ValueError(f"Bad edge {u}->{v}") if not vis[v]: stack.append(v) if len(stack) > max_stack: max_stack = len(stack) return out, pops, max_stack

That’s the kind of instrumentation I need. Another bug: I wrote a function to compute reachable nodes from multiple roots, but it’s slower than doing them separately. I expected sharing work would help. Here’s what I did:
def multi_reach(adj, roots): n = len(adj) vis = bytearray(n) stack = list(roots) while stack: u = stack.pop() if vis[u]: continue vis[u] = 1 stack.extend(adj[u]) return vis
Why might this be slower?

A few reasons: 1) Roots may be mostly disjoint. Doing them separately lets each traversal have a smaller visited array if you allocate per component? But if you always allocate n-sized visited anyway, that doesn’t apply. 2) The main cost is scanning edges of visited nodes. Your multi_reach scans each visited node’s adjacency once, which is optimal. So if it’s slower, it’s likely due to overhead elsewhere: converting roots, building the initial stack, or post-processing vis. 3) If you compare against “separate runs” that stop early (e.g., each root traversal has its own visited and doesn’t traverse into regions already covered by earlier roots because those earlier traversals mutated/pruned the graph), then the work differs. 4) Cache effects and Python overhead: a single huge run might keep the stack large and cause more reallocations; multiple smaller runs might have smaller stacks and better locality. To diagnose: count the number of nodes visited and edges scanned in both approaches. If multi_reach visits more nodes, it’s doing more work (maybe because separate runs start with a graph that has already been pruned/filtered per root). Also, consider deduplicating roots first: roots = list({r for r in roots if 0 <= r < n}) And pushing neighbors only if not visited (which you already do only on pop; you could reduce stack growth by checking before push): for v in adj[u]: if not vis[v]: stack.append(v) This is often faster because it reduces stack size significantly in graphs with converging edges.
I didn’t do the pre-check before pushing; I’ll add it. Now, I’m also using a reverse adjacency for some analysis, and building it is expensive. Any memory/time tips?

For reverse adjacency (incoming edges), cost is O(E) time and memory. Tips: - If you only need reverse neighbors for nodes you actually visit from some root, build reverse adjacency lazily for the reachable subgraph: first collect reachable nodes, then build reverse lists only for edges among them. - Use arrays of arrays with preallocation if you know indegrees. Two-pass: 1) Count indegree per node. 2) Allocate exact-sized lists (or arrays) and fill. In pure Python lists-of-lists, exact sizing is tricky but you can precreate lists with capacity by using list multiplication of placeholders and fill by index; however, dynamic append is usually fine. - If memory is tight, store edges in a single flat array with offsets (CSR/CSC style). That’s more work but very memory efficient. A simple two-pass in Python: def build_rev(adj): n = len(adj) rev = [[] for _ in range(n)] for u in range(n): for v in adj[u]: rev[v].append(u) return rev If you need speed, localize variables: def build_rev(adj): n = len(adj) rev = [[] for _ in range(n)] rev_append = [lst.append for lst in rev] for u, nbrs in enumerate(adj): for v in nbrs: rev_append[v](u) return rev This micro-optimization can matter at tens of millions of edges.
Helpful. Another debugging thing: sometimes my graph has nodes with extremely large outdegree, and DFS stack spikes and memory blows up. Would BFS be better?

BFS usually makes memory worse when there’s high branching, because it holds an entire frontier. DFS holds a path plus some siblings, but with “push all neighbors” DFS can still spike if a node has huge outdegree because you push them all at once. Mitigations: 1) Use neighbor iteration without pushing all at once by storing an iterator state on the stack: - stack holds (u, next_index) and you advance one neighbor at a time. That keeps stack size closer to depth, not outdegree. 2) If you don’t need strict DFS order, you can chunk pushing: push neighbors in slices, but that’s awkward. Iterator-state DFS (memory friendlier for huge degrees): def dfs_iter_state(adj, start): n = len(adj) vis = bytearray(n) out = [] stack = [(start, 0)] while stack: u, i = stack[-1] if not vis[u]: vis[u] = 1 out.append(u) nbrs = adj[u] if i >= len(nbrs): stack.pop() continue v = nbrs[i] stack[-1] = (u, i+1) if not vis[v]: stack.append((v, 0)) return out This avoids ever storing all neighbors on the stack at once; you only store the current index for each active node.
That’s great. I didn’t realize pushing neighbors was the spike. For postorder DP (like height), can I do the same iterator-state approach?

Yes. You can do a single-stack postorder with iterator state and compute DP on exit. For DAG/tree (no cycles), something like: def height_postorder_iter(adj, root): n = len(adj) vis = bytearray(n)      # optional for DAG; for tree you can skip on_path = bytearray(n)  # cycle detection dp = [0]*n stack = [(root, 0)] while stack: u, i = stack[-1] if not on_path[u]: if on_path[u]: raise ValueError("cycle")  # not reachable here on_path[u] = 1 nbrs = adj[u] if i < len(nbrs): v = nbrs[i] stack[-1] = (u, i+1) if on_path[v]: raise ValueError("cycle detected") # For DAG with shared nodes: if dp[v] already computed, you can skip descending. if dp[v] == 0: stack.append((v, 0)) continue # exit u: all children processed if not nbrs: dp[u] = 1 else: best = 0 for v in nbrs: if dp[v] > best: best = dp[v] dp[u] = 1 + best on_path[u] = 0 stack.pop() return dp[root] But note: for DAGs, “dp[v] == 0” is not a safe “uncomputed” sentinel if true dp could be 0. Here dp is at least 1, so 0 works. Also, for DAGs with multiple parents, this single-stack DFS order will compute dp for nodes when first encountered; that’s okay because dp depends only on descendants, and those are computed before exit. It still works, but it’s doing a DFS, not a full topo DP; it’s fine if you start from a single root and want values only for reachable nodes. For general DAG with shared nodes, you must ensure you don’t prematurely compute dp[u] before all its children are computed. The DFS guarantees that: you only compute on exit, after processing all children. If a child was already computed via another branch, dp[child] is available.
I see. For cycle detection in that DP, using on_path bytearray is fine. But how do I report the actual cycle path in an iterative DP like that?

To report a cycle path, you need parent pointers in the traversal stack. With iterator-state DFS, you already have the stack of nodes. When you see an edge u -> v where v is on_path, you can extract the cycle from the stack: - Find v in the stack nodes, then slice from its position to end, plus v. Implementation detail: stack currently holds tuples (node, index). Create a mapping from node->position for nodes on the stack, updated as you push/pop, so you don’t do O(depth) search each time. Sketch: pos = [-1]*n  # position in stack, -1 if not on stack When pushing node x: pos[x] = len(stack) When popping: pos[u] = -1 When encountering v with pos[v] != -1: cycle_nodes = [stack[i][0] for i in range(pos[v], len(stack))] + [v] Then convert to labels if needed.
Great. I’ll implement that. Another question: I’m using pytest, and sometimes I can’t reproduce a failure unless I run the whole suite. Any debugging tips specific to state leakage?

For state leakage, the key is to identify hidden globals/mutable defaults/caches. Checklist: - Search for function definitions with defaults like [], {}, set(), bytearray(), dict(). - Search for module-level mutable objects that tests might mutate. - Look for lru_cache usage: caches persist across tests unless cleared. In pytest, you can call depth.cache_clear() in a fixture. - If you use randomization, set a seed and print it when failing. - Run with -k to bisect which test order triggers it; pytest has plugins and options like --maxfail=1 -x and also you can use pytest-randomly to randomize order; if randomizing reveals order-dependence, that screams shared state. A very practical tactic: add an autouse fixture that checks caches are empty or clears them: import pytest @pytest.fixture(autouse=True) def clear_caches(): yield depth.cache_clear() And avoid using module-level singletons for visited arrays or accumulators.
That’s useful. I do have some @lru_cache functions. I didn’t think about clearing them. Also, I’m using a global mapping idx and labels_list in the module for speed. Is that safe?

It’s safe if you treat them as immutable after construction. Problems arise if: - You rebuild them in one test and not in another, but functions reference the old globals. - You mutate labels_list or idx (accidentally). - You have multiple graphs in the same process but the globals correspond to only one of them. If your tests build different graphs, using module-level idx/labels_list is risky. Better: wrap graph + mappings into a small object and pass it around, or return them from a builder: class Graph: def __init__(self, adj, labels, idx): self.adj = adj self.labels = labels self.idx = idx Then functions take a Graph instance. If you keep globals, at least ensure each test rebuilds them fresh and doesn’t rely on prior state.

I’ll encapsulate into a Graph class. Now, I have one more tricky issue: I want to store per-node metadata computed during traversal (like depth, parent in spanning tree). With millions of nodes, dicts are heavy. What’s the best approach?

If nodes are 0..n-1 ints, use parallel arrays (Python lists) for metadata: - parent = [-1]*n - depth = [-1]*n (or array('i') if you want tighter storage) - color/visited = bytearray(n) During traversal: parent[v] = u depth[v] = depth[u] + 1 For BFS shortest paths in unweighted graphs, BFS is appropriate and uses a queue; for DFS spanning tree, use stack. If memory is very tight, consider: - array('i') / array('l') for integers (more compact than Python int list, but still stores Python ints when you assign? Actually array stores C-type values, so it is compact). Example: from array import array parent = array('i', [-1]) * n  # careful: this repeats values correctly for arrays depth = array('i', [-1]) * n But depth might exceed 2^31-1 in pathological cases; for safety use 'l' or 'q' if needed.
For BFS, I used collections.deque and it’s okay. For DFS, I used list as stack. Any micro-optimizations worth it?

The biggest wins in Python graph loops are usually: - Use local variables for hot names (adj_local = adj, vis_local = vis, stack_append = stack.append, stack_pop = stack.pop). - Avoid repeated global lookups. - Avoid pushing already-visited neighbors (reduces stack pressure). - Store adjacency as lists of ints, not generators. - If you can, use PyPy or C extensions for extreme sizes, but that’s beyond the immediate scope. Example tuned DFS: def dfs_count(adj, start): n = len(adj) vis = bytearray(n) stack = [start] vis_local = vis adj_local = adj pop = stack.pop append = stack.append count = 0 while stack: u = pop() if vis_local[u]: continue vis_local[u] = 1 count += 1 for v in adj_local[u]: if not vis_local[v]: append(v) return count This can be noticeably faster.
Great. I’ll implement these. If I run into another RecursionError, it’s probably from repr/eq or hidden recursion elsewhere, right?

Yes. At this point, if your traversal is iterative, remaining RecursionError sources are commonly: - __repr__ or logging of recursive structures - dataclass-generated __eq__ or custom __eq__ walking children/parent - recursive property methods (e.g., node.path() calls parent.path()) - accidental recursion via callbacks or visitor patterns When it happens, look at the traceback: it will show which function is recursing. If it’s not your traversal, it’s usually repr/eq. If you paste a traceback, we can pinpoint it quickly.
I’ll do that next time. For now, I’m going to fix the bytearray default, remove -1 mapping, and add cycle reporting with pos[] on the stack. Thanks—this was very actionable.

Good plan. When you have the cycle path output, if you share one example (as labels), I can help interpret whether it’s a real data issue or a conversion/adjacency bug, and suggest where in your pipeline to enforce the DAG constraint.
When should I use git rebase instead of merge? I keep hearing conflicting advice.

Rebase and merge both combine changes from one line of development into another, but they do it by producing different history shapes and different guarantees. - git merge: takes two branch tips and creates a new merge commit that has both as parents (unless it’s a fast-forward merge). It preserves the exact historical topology: “these commits happened on this branch, then we merged.” - git rebase: takes a sequence of commits and “replays” them onto a new base, creating new commits with new IDs. It produces a linear history (or at least a different topology) by rewriting history. When to prefer which depends on what you’re optimizing for: Use merge when: 1) You want to preserve the true branching history (e.g., you want to show that two lines of work were developed in parallel). 2) The commits are already shared with others or are part of a public history you don’t want to rewrite. 3) You want to avoid rewriting commit IDs (e.g., for references in discussions, tags, or external tooling that points to specific SHAs). Use rebase when: 1) You want a clean, linear story for a set of commits before integrating them, and those commits are not “public” yet (only in your local branch or otherwise safe to rewrite). 2) You want to avoid merge commits in the main line and prefer a straight line of commits. 3) You want to update your branch on top of the latest base branch to resolve conflicts earlier and keep a simpler final history. A practical rule: rebase your local work to keep it up-to-date; merge to integrate completed work, especially when history is already shared. If you tell me what your typical flow looks like (e.g., you have a main branch and you branch off it, then bring changes back), I can give concrete commands and examples.

I mostly have a main branch and I create a feature branch, commit a bunch, then eventually I want those changes in main. But I also need to pull updates from main while I'm working. That's where I get lost.

That’s the classic scenario where both merge and rebase can work, but they feel different. Let’s name things: - main: the base branch you ultimately want to integrate into - feature: your branch where you commit changes While you’re working, main continues to move forward (new commits appear). You want your feature branch to incorporate those updates so you’re not drifting too far. Two ways to “bring main into feature”: A) Merge main into feature (non-rewriting) 1. You’re on feature 2. git fetch origin 3. git merge origin/main This creates a merge commit on feature (unless it’s fast-forward, which it usually won’t be if you have feature commits). History now shows a merge bubble: feature contains its commits plus a merge commit that brings in main. Pros: - No rewriting, safe even if feature commits are shared. - Accurately records “I merged main into feature at this point.” Cons: - Feature history can get noisy with repeated merge commits. B) Rebase feature onto main (rewriting) 1. You’re on feature 2. git fetch origin 3. git rebase origin/main This takes your feature commits that are “on top of” the old base and replays them on top of the latest main. The feature branch tip moves, and all your feature commits get new SHAs. Pros: - Feature history stays linear; no merge commits from main. - When you eventually integrate feature into main, you often get a clean fast-forward or a clean set of commits. Cons: - Rewrites history (new SHAs). If you already pushed feature somewhere and other clones reference those old SHAs, you need to be careful. Then there’s how you “bring feature into main” at the end: - Merge feature into main: git checkout main; git merge feature (creates a merge commit unless fast-forward). - Rebase-and-fast-forward (or “linear merge”): rebase feature onto main first, then fast-forward merge. - Squash merge: combine feature commits into one commit on main. If you want, we can walk through a concrete example with commit graphs and exact commands, including what you’d see in git log --graph.
Yes, please. I always get confused by the graphs. Can you show an example with actual commits like A, B, C?

Sure. I’ll use letters for commits and show parent relationships. Start: main has two commits: main: A - B You branch off at B: feature: (starts at B) You add two commits on feature: feature: A - B - C - D main:    A - B Meanwhile, main gets two new commits: main: A - B - E - F feature: A - B - C - D Now you want to “incorporate main into feature” while continuing to work. Option 1: Merge main into feature You’re on feature: git merge main Result: main:    A - B - E - F \ feature: A - B - C - D - M / (where M is a merge commit whose parents are D and F) Graphically: A--B--E--F \      \ C--D---M Now feature contains everything from main plus your commits, but with a merge commit M. Option 2: Rebase feature onto main You’re on feature: git rebase main Git finds the fork point (B), takes commits C and D, and replays them onto F. New commits are created, call them C' and D' (new SHAs). Result: main:    A - B - E - F feature:             C' - D' Graph: A--B--E--F--C'--D' Notice: the old C and D still exist in your repository for a while (as dangling commits), but feature now points to D' instead of D. That’s the “history rewrite.” Then at the end, to integrate feature into main: - After merge approach: merging feature into main would create another merge commit (unless you do some fast-forward trick, but typically it’s another merge commit). - After rebase approach: main can fast-forward to D' (if you haven’t made more commits on main since rebasing), producing a straight line. The important mental model: - Merge combines two tips by creating a new commit with two parents. - Rebase moves (copies) commits to a new base. Want to see what happens if you already pushed feature, then rebased and tried to push again?
Yes, that's another thing. I push feature, then later I rebase and push and it yells at me about non-fast-forward. What exactly is happening?

That error is Git protecting you from rewriting history on the remote branch. Scenario: 1) You push feature when it has commits C and D (SHAs based on their parents): remote feature: A - B - C - D 2) Later, main advances to E - F, and you rebase feature onto main. Your local feature becomes: local feature: A - B - E - F - C' - D' Now the remote branch still points to D, but your local branch points to D'. These histories are not the same line: D' is not a descendant of D (it’s a different commit even if the patch content is similar). So when you run: git push origin feature Git checks: “Is the remote tip (D) an ancestor of what you’re trying to push (D')?” If yes, it’s a fast-forward update and is safe. Here, it’s not, because D and D' diverge. So Git rejects it with a non-fast-forward error. To update the remote to match your rebased branch, you must force push, because you’re asking the remote to move its branch pointer backwards/sideways to a different history: Safer force push: git push --force-with-lease origin feature --force-with-lease says: “Force push, but only if the remote branch still points to what I think it points to.” It prevents you from clobbering someone else’s new commits on that remote branch. So what’s happening conceptually: - Before rebase: remote has C and D. - After rebase: you created new commits C' and D'. The old commits C and D are no longer on your branch tip. - A normal push can only move the remote forward along the same lineage; it can’t replace history. - Force push replaces the remote branch ref to point at your new tip. If you want to avoid force pushes entirely, you can choose “merge main into feature” instead of rebasing after pushing. Or you can only rebase before the first push.
I see. But sometimes I want a clean history on main. People say "never rebase" and others say "always rebase". Is there a more nuanced rule?

A nuanced rule is mostly about whether the commits you’re rebasing are considered “published and depended on.” A practical guideline: 1) Rebase is great for local, unpublished, or disposable history. - You’re still shaping your commits. - You want to reorder, squash, edit messages, split commits, etc. - You want to keep your branch up-to-date without merge commits. 2) Merge is safer for published history. - If a commit SHA is already referenced by others (someone based work on it, or it’s in a shared branch that others fetch), rewriting it creates confusion and extra work. - Merge avoids changing existing SHAs. But “published” doesn’t have to mean a big social context; it can just mean: “Does any other clone or process rely on these SHAs?” If yes, avoid rewriting unless you know exactly what you’re doing. For main itself: - Many people treat main as immutable history: no rebases, no force pushes. - Integration into main can still be linear without rebasing main: you can rebase feature before merging, then fast-forward or do a rebase-and-merge strategy that doesn’t rewrite main’s existing commits. Additionally, there’s a middle ground: - Keep main as merge commits (preserve branching). - Keep feature branches rebased during development. - At integration time, either merge with a merge commit (preserve feature boundary) or squash merge (one commit) depending on how you want history to look. If you want, we can compare three end states on main: merge commit, rebase-and-fast-forward, and squash.
Yes, compare those. I never really understood what squash does compared to rebase.

Let’s reuse the earlier example. Starting point: main: A - B - E - F feature (based on B): C - D (with B as base) So before integration, if you rebased feature you’d have: main: A - B - E - F feature:             C' - D' Now, three ways to integrate: 1) Merge commit (preserve branch structure) If you merge feature into main without forcing linear history: git checkout main git merge feature Result: A - B - E - F - M2 \   / C' - D' M2 is a merge commit with two parents: F and D'. Pros: - Keeps a record that “these commits were integrated together.” - If there were conflicts, the merge commit records the resolution in a single place. Cons: - Adds merge commits; history is non-linear. 2) Rebase-and-fast-forward (linear integration without squashing) If feature is already rebased onto main (C', D' sit on top of F), then: git checkout main git merge --ff-only feature (or just git merge feature if fast-forward is possible and your config allows it) Result: A - B - E - F - C' - D' Pros: - Main stays linear. - You keep each commit as its own commit (good for bisect, blame, incremental review). Cons: - You lose the explicit “feature boundary” unless you add tags or rely on commit messages. 3) Squash merge (combine all feature commits into one on main) You keep feature as-is (either rebased or not; squash doesn’t require rebasing), then: git checkout main git merge --squash feature git commit -m "Add feature X" Result: A - B - E - F - S Where S is a new commit that contains the net effect of C and D, but not the individual commits. Pros: - Very clean main history (one commit per logical change). - Avoids messy intermediate commits like “fix typo” or “wip.” Cons: - You lose individual commit granularity (harder to bisect within the feature). - The original commits still exist on the feature branch, but main doesn’t have them. Key difference between squash and rebase: - Rebase rewrites commits but keeps them as separate commits (unless you use interactive rebase to squash). - Squash merge creates a new commit representing the combined diff, without preserving the original commit identities on main.
How does interactive rebase fit in? Is it just for cleaning up commit messages?

Interactive rebase is a general-purpose history editing tool. Cleaning up messages is one use, but it can do much more. You run something like: git rebase -i <base> Git opens a todo list of commits from after <base> up to HEAD, in order. You can: - reword: change commit message - edit: stop and let you amend the commit content - squash/fixup: combine commits (squash keeps both messages; fixup discards the later message) - drop: remove a commit entirely - reorder lines: reorder commits (careful: can cause conflicts or change meaning) - exec: run a command after each commit (e.g., run tests) Example: Suppose feature has commits: C: "WIP" D: "Add parser" E: "Fix parser bug" F: "Formatting" You might do: git rebase -i main And change the todo list to: pick D Add parser fixup E Fix parser bug fixup C WIP drop F Formatting This would produce a single clean commit “Add parser” that includes the bug fix and whatever was in WIP, and it would drop the formatting commit if it’s not desired (though dropping formatting is often questionable; just an example). Interactive rebase is powerful, but the same warning applies: it rewrites commits. Use it before those commits are widely depended on.
Okay. Another detail: what's the difference between "git pull --rebase" and doing fetch + rebase?

Conceptually they’re the same operation; pull is just a convenience that combines fetching and integrating. - git fetch: updates your remote-tracking branches (e.g., origin/main, origin/feature) without changing your local branches. - git pull: does git fetch, then integrates the fetched changes into your current branch (either by merge or by rebase, depending on options/config). So: git pull --rebase is roughly: git fetch git rebase @{u} where @{u} means the upstream tracking branch of your current branch (e.g., origin/main if you’re on main, or origin/feature if you’re on feature and it tracks origin/feature). Some nuances: - git pull --rebase typically stashes and re-applies local uncommitted changes automatically (depending on config like rebase.autoStash). - fetch + rebase gives you more explicit control and visibility (you can inspect what changed after fetch before rebasing). If you want a reliable workflow with fewer surprises, many people prefer: git fetch --prune git rebase origin/main because it’s explicit about the base you’re rebasing onto.
Wait, why would I rebase onto origin/main instead of main?

Good question. It’s about ensuring you’re rebasing onto the latest known state from the remote, not whatever your local main currently is. If you do: git rebase main and your local main is behind (you haven’t fetched/pulled), then you rebase onto an outdated base. You might think you’re up-to-date, but you aren’t. A robust sequence is: git fetch origin git rebase origin/main This guarantees you’re using the newest remote-tracking reference. Alternatively, you can keep local main updated: git checkout main git pull --ff-only then go back to feature: git checkout feature git rebase main That works too, but it’s more steps. Using origin/main directly avoids needing to update local main just to use it as a base.
If rebase replays commits one by one, how does it handle conflicts? Is it worse than merge conflicts?

Conflicts can happen in both, but they show up differently. Merge conflict behavior: - When you merge two branches, Git tries to merge the final snapshots plus the common ancestor. - If conflicts occur, you resolve them once for the merge commit (though the conflict can be complex because it’s the combined effect of many commits). Rebase conflict behavior: - Git applies your commits one at a time onto the new base. - If a particular commit doesn’t apply cleanly, rebase stops at that commit and asks you to resolve conflicts. - You may have to resolve conflicts multiple times if multiple commits touch the same areas in ways that conflict with the new base. Is it worse? Not necessarily: - Rebase can make conflicts smaller and more localized to the specific commit that introduced a change, which can be easier to reason about. - But if your commit series is long and touches the same lines repeatedly, you might resolve similar conflicts repeatedly. Commands during rebase conflicts: - Fix files, git add them - git rebase --continue If you want to abandon: - git rebase --abort If you want to skip the problematic commit (dangerous if it matters): - git rebase --skip With merge conflicts: - Fix files, git add - git commit (the merge commit), or git merge --continue on newer Git versions So rebase conflicts are more iterative; merge conflicts are more “all at once.”
What is "rerere"? I saw it mentioned as helping with repeated conflicts.

rerere stands for “reuse recorded resolution.” When enabled, Git records how you resolved a conflict and can automatically apply the same resolution if it sees the same conflict again. This is especially helpful with: - Rebase where similar conflicts appear across multiple commits - Repeated merges/rebases where the same conflict patterns recur Enable it: git config --global rerere.enabled true How it works in practice: 1) You hit a conflict during rebase/merge. 2) You resolve it manually and stage the result. 3) Git stores the before/after for that conflict. 4) Next time Git encounters the same conflict (same conflict markers context), it can apply your previous resolution automatically. It’s not magic; it won’t solve all conflicts, but it can dramatically reduce repetitive conflict work.
Let's talk about "fast-forward" merges. I kind of get it, but when does it happen and why is it special?

A fast-forward merge happens when the branch you’re merging into has not diverged—i.e., its tip is an ancestor of the branch you’re merging. Example: main: A - B feature: A - B - C - D If you merge feature into main: git checkout main git merge feature Since main’s tip B is an ancestor of D, Git can “fast-forward” main to point to D. No merge commit is needed; it’s just moving the branch pointer. Result: main: A - B - C - D feature: (still points to D too) Why it’s special: - No merge commit is created. - History remains linear. - It’s effectively the same as “I just advanced main to include those commits.” Fast-forward is often desirable if you want a linear history and you’re okay not recording a merge boundary. You can control behavior: - git merge --ff-only: fail if it can’t fast-forward (prevents accidental merge commits) - git merge --no-ff: always create a merge commit even if fast-forward is possible (forces a merge commit boundary)
So if I rebase my feature onto main, does that guarantee a fast-forward merge later?

It increases the chance, but it’s not an absolute guarantee unless nothing else changes on main after the rebase. If you do: - main at F - feature rebased onto F, ending at D' Then at that moment, merging feature into main can fast-forward, because main is an ancestor of feature. But if main advances again after you rebased (say main gets G and H), then main is no longer an ancestor of your feature tip. You’d need to rebase again (or merge) to regain fast-forward ability. So the guarantee is conditional: - If feature is based on the current tip of main and main hasn’t moved since, then a fast-forward merge is possible.
That makes sense. I also hear about "rebase --onto". What's that for?

git rebase --onto is for moving a range of commits from one base to another base, especially when the branch structure is more complex than “feature branched from main.” General form: git rebase --onto <newbase> <upstream> <branch> Meaning: - Take commits reachable from <branch> but not from <upstream> - Replay them onto <newbase> Example scenario: You have: main: A - B - C feature:        C - D - E but you realize feature should have been based on B, not C, or perhaps on some other branch. Or more common: You have a stacked branch: main: A - B - C topic1:      C - D - E topic2:            E - F - G If you want to rebase topic2 onto main (skipping topic1 commits), you can: git rebase --onto main topic1 topic2 This takes F and G (commits on topic2 not in topic1) and replays them onto main. It’s also used to extract part of a branch: - Suppose feature has commits C D E F, and you want to move E F to a new base, leaving C D behind. You can specify upstream as the commit before E. It’s a precision tool for “move these commits to there.”
How do I find the right upstream commit for --onto? I always mess that up.

The safest approach is to identify the exact commit ranges with git log and/or git merge-base. Techniques: 1) Use git merge-base to find the fork point If you want the common ancestor between feature and main: git merge-base feature main That outputs the commit SHA where they diverged (the best common ancestor). Then you can rebase: git rebase main feature (which implicitly uses that merge-base), but for --onto you might use it as <upstream>. 2) Use commit ranges in git log - git log main..feature shows commits reachable from feature but not main (i.e., what you’d be rebasing/merging). - git log feature --not main is similar. - git log --graph --oneline --decorate --all helps visualize. 3) Use relative refs - HEAD~3 means “3 commits before HEAD” - feature~2 etc. For --onto: - <upstream> is usually the old base you want to exclude. - <newbase> is where you want those commits to land. Example: If feature was based on X but you want it based on Y: git rebase --onto Y X feature If you’re not sure, you can dry-run mentally by listing commits: git log --oneline X..feature Those are the commits that will be replayed.
Is there a way to "undo" a rebase if I messed it up?

Yes, usually. Git keeps a lot of safety nets. 1) git rebase --abort (if the rebase is currently in progress) This returns you to the state before the rebase started. 2) Use reflog (if the rebase already finished) Git records where HEAD and branch refs used to point. Run: git reflog You’ll see entries like: feature@{0}: rebase finished: returning to refs/heads/feature feature@{1}: rebase: commit message... feature@{n}: checkout: moving from ... And importantly, you’ll see the old HEAD position before rebase started. To reset branch back: git reset --hard <old-sha> Example: git reset --hard feature@{3} (where feature@{3} is the state before rebase) Caution: --hard discards uncommitted changes. If you have uncommitted work, stash it first. So you can almost always recover from a botched rebase using reflog.
I've also seen "git pull --rebase --autostash". What's autostash doing?

autostash temporarily stashes your uncommitted working tree changes before starting the rebase (or pull --rebase), then reapplies them afterward. It’s useful when: - You have local modifications not yet committed - You want to update/rebase without manually stashing Mechanism: 1) Git creates a stash entry (like git stash push) 2) Performs the rebase 3) Applies the stash (like git stash pop) Potential issues: - The stash application can conflict if your local uncommitted changes overlap with changes introduced by the rebase. - If something goes wrong, the stash might not be dropped automatically (which is good—your changes aren’t lost, but you may need to apply it manually). You can set it globally: git config --global rebase.autoStash true
Sometimes I do a merge and then I regret it and want to "unmerge". Is that similar to undoing rebase?

Similar in that reflog can save you, but there are multiple cases. Case A: Merge in progress (conflicts unresolved) - You can abort: git merge --abort This returns to pre-merge state. Case B: Merge completed, not pushed anywhere - You can reset branch pointer back using reflog: git reset --hard HEAD@{1} (or find the pre-merge commit in git reflog) Case C: Merge completed and you want to undo it in a history-preserving way - Use git revert on the merge commit: git revert -m 1 <merge-commit-sha> The -m 1 chooses which parent is the “mainline” to keep. This creates a new commit that undoes the changes introduced by the merge without rewriting history. Reset rewrites local history (moves the branch pointer). Revert creates a new commit that negates changes.
Okay, I'm trying to understand commit IDs changing. If rebased commits have different SHAs, does that break anything?

It can, depending on what references those SHAs. What changes: - A commit SHA is a hash of the commit contents plus metadata, including its parent commit SHA. When you rebase, the parent changes, so the SHA changes even if the diff is identical. What can break: - Links or references to specific SHAs (e.g., you noted a SHA somewhere) - Any downstream work based on the old commits (someone else or another branch built on top of them) - Some tooling that keys off SHAs (local scripts, build caches, etc., depending on your setup) - Signed commits: rebasing invalidates existing signatures because the commit changes; you’d need to re-sign. What usually doesn’t break: - The code state (you still end up with the same content, typically) - Tags/branches that you update intentionally - If nobody else has the old SHAs and nothing depends on them, it’s fine. This is why “don’t rebase public history” is a common guideline: once SHAs are shared and depended on, rewriting them creates extra reconciliation work.
You mentioned signed commits. How does rebase interact with GPG signing?

If commits are signed (GPG or SSH signing), the signature is part of the commit object. Rebase creates new commit objects, so the old signatures don’t carry over. If you want rebased commits to be signed, you can: - Re-sign during rebase by setting: git config --global commit.gpgsign true and/or use: git rebase --exec 'git commit --amend --no-edit -S' But that’s heavy. More commonly: - You sign the final commits you care about (e.g., after interactive rebase cleanup). - Or you avoid rewriting signed commits. There’s also: git rebase --rebase-merges and some options to preserve merges, but signing still changes because the commit objects change.
What's "rebase --rebase-merges"? I thought rebase flattens merges.

By default, rebase takes a linear series of commits and replays them; merge commits typically get flattened away (it tries to create a linear history). git rebase --rebase-merges (older alias: --preserve-merges, but that one is deprecated) tells Git to try to preserve the branching structure within the rebased range. It recreates merge commits as part of the rebase. When it’s useful: - Your branch contains merge commits that represent meaningful structure (e.g., you merged a sub-branch into your branch and want to keep that grouping). - You want to move an entire branch that includes merges onto a new base without losing merge topology. Caveats: - It’s more complex and can lead to more conflicts or confusing results. - The recreated merges are still new commits (new SHAs). If you don’t need internal merges, it’s often simpler to avoid merging within a feature branch and just rebase frequently, but that’s a preference.
If I want to keep main history linear, is it better to always rebase my feature branch and then fast-forward merge?

That’s a coherent approach: - While working: rebase feature onto main to keep it current and linear. - Before integrating: interactive rebase to clean up commit series. - Integrate by fast-forwarding main to feature (or equivalent). This yields a linear main history with individual commits preserved. But tradeoffs: - If you value explicit merge boundaries, you lose that unless you use merge commits. - If you do want boundaries but also linearity, you can approximate boundaries by using annotated tags or by ensuring commit messages include a consistent prefix, but it’s not the same as a merge commit. - If you have long-running feature branches with lots of rebases, you’ll be rewriting a lot, which can be annoying if you have multiple clones or CI references. Another popular approach for linear main is squash merging: main becomes a sequence of “feature commits” each representing a logical change, but you lose fine-grained history on main.
How does git bisect behave with merge commits vs linear history?

git bisect works best when each commit is a meaningful, buildable state. Linear history helps because bisect walks along a single path. With merge commits: - Bisect still works, but merges can complicate the search because the “first bad commit” might be a merge commit whose parents are good independently, but the combination is bad. - Bisect typically chooses commits along a first-parent path depending on options, but merges introduce multiple parents and can affect how “midpoints” are chosen. If you keep main linear (via rebase+ff or squash), bisect is straightforward: every commit has one parent, and you’re searching a simple chain. However, even with linear history, if intermediate commits are broken (e.g., “WIP commit breaks build”), bisect becomes painful. That’s why interactive rebase to clean up and ensure each commit is coherent can matter more than merge-vs-rebase itself.
What about blame? People say rebasing makes blame nicer?

git blame attributes lines to commits. “Nicer blame” usually means: - Fewer “noise commits” like “fix formatting” or “oops” - Commits that reflect logical changes, with clear messages Rebase can help because you can edit/squash/reorder commits before they land on main, leading to better-structured commits. But merge can also be fine if the commits themselves are clean. One subtlety: - If you squash merge, blame will point to the squash commit for all lines introduced by that feature, even if they were introduced across multiple commits originally. That’s sometimes okay, sometimes not. - If you rebase and keep separate commits, blame can point to the specific commit that introduced a line, which can be more informative. So it’s less “rebase vs merge” and more “commit hygiene vs messy history.”
I have another confusion: If I merge main into feature repeatedly, then later merge feature back into main, does that create weird extra merges?

It can create a more complex graph, but Git is designed to handle it. The “weirdness” is mainly about readability. Example: - You merge main into feature multiple times: feature gets merge commits M1, M2, ... - When you finally merge feature into main, Git looks at the commit graph and will incorporate whatever commits main doesn’t already have. Since feature already contains main’s commits via those earlier merges, the final merge might be relatively straightforward, but the graph will show nested merges. This doesn’t duplicate content; Git uses commit ancestry to avoid reapplying changes. But the history can look busy: - Many merge commits on feature - One final merge commit on main If you care about a clean history graph, rebasing feature onto main periodically avoids those intermediate merge commits.
Is there any risk that rebasing changes the code compared to merging?

In principle, both should result in the same final content if you resolve conflicts equivalently. But there are subtle cases: - Conflict resolution choices: With merge, you resolve conflicts once with both sides’ final states in mind. With rebase, you resolve conflicts per commit. It’s possible to resolve conflicts differently in each approach, leading to different final code. - Non-commutative changes: If commits depend on earlier context, replaying them in a new base might expose issues.
