But Git applies patches with context; if it applies cleanly, the result should be what you expect. - Reordering commits during interactive rebase can change results if commits are not independent. - If you have merges in your feature branch and you rebase without preserving merges, the flattening can change how conflict resolutions are represented. So: rebase doesn’t inherently “change code,” but it changes how changes are applied, which can lead to different conflict resolutions or outcomes if you’re not careful.
I want to practice reading git log graphs. What commands do you recommend?

A few useful ones: 1) Compact graph view: git log --graph --oneline --decorate --all - --graph shows ASCII graph - --oneline shortens each commit - --decorate shows branch/tag names - --all shows all refs (so you can see where branches are) 2) Show only current branch, but with merges: git log --graph --oneline --decorate 3) Show commits in feature not in main: git log --oneline main..feature 4) Show commits in main not in feature: git log --oneline feature..main 5) Show first-parent history (useful for main with merges): git log --first-parent --oneline --decorate If you’re learning graphs, #1 is the go-to. It can be busy, but it builds intuition fast.
Sometimes I see "origin/HEAD" in decorate output. What is that?

origin/HEAD is a symbolic reference on your local repo that points to the default branch of the remote named origin. It’s like: “When I refer to origin without specifying a branch, what’s the default?” It often points to origin/main (or origin/master depending on naming). You can see it with: git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD or: git remote show origin It’s informational; you usually don’t need to manipulate it directly.
If I rebase feature onto main, and I had already merged some other branch into feature, does that get lost?

By default, yes: a standard rebase will linearize and can “lose” the merge commit structure, though it doesn’t lose the changes (it replays the resulting commits). But the explicit merge commits and the separate branch identity may not be preserved. Example: feature includes a merge commit bringing in some sub-branch. A normal rebase will typically turn the combined history into a linear list of commits, potentially changing how that integration is represented. If you want to preserve those merges, use: git rebase --rebase-merges main This attempts to recreate the merge topology. But ask yourself: do you need the internal merge history, or do you just need the final content? Often, if the internal merge structure isn’t important, flattening is fine and simpler.
I've heard of "cherry-pick" too. Is rebase basically a bunch of cherry-picks?

That’s a good mental model. - git cherry-pick <commit> applies the changes introduced by that commit onto your current HEAD, creating a new commit. - git rebase takes a range of commits and effectively cherry-picks them in order onto a new base. So, yes: rebase is like automating a sequence of cherry-picks, plus some bookkeeping (like updating branch pointers, handling conflicts, and possibly preserving merges with --rebase-merges). This also explains why commit SHAs change: cherry-picking creates new commits.
If rebase is like cherry-pick, what's the difference between merging and cherry-picking?

Merge integrates histories: it combines the full ancestry of another branch into your branch, preserving where commits came from and connecting the graphs. It brings all commits (as commits) into the history. Cherry-pick integrates changes: it takes specific commits’ diffs and applies them, creating new commits on the target branch. It does not connect the histories; it copies changes. Consequences: - Merge preserves original commit identities and ancestry. - Cherry-pick duplicates commits (new SHAs) and can lead to duplicate changes if you later merge branches that already share some changes but not commit ancestry (Git can often detect duplicates via patch-ID in some cases, but not always). Cherry-pick is great for: - Applying a specific fix commit to another branch - Selecting a subset of commits Merge is better for: - Integrating whole lines of development Rebase sits closer to cherry-pick (copy commits) but is used to move a whole branch’s commits onto a new base.
Duplicate changes sounds scary. How does Git avoid applying the same change twice?

Git primarily reasons about commits and ancestry, not about “changes” as abstract diffs. If you merge branches that share commits in their history, Git can see the common ancestors and won’t duplicate. If you cherry-pick a commit from one branch to another, you create a new commit with a different SHA. Now you have two different commits that introduce similar or identical changes. If you later merge the branches, Git might attempt to merge both lineages. Often it won’t literally apply the same lines twice because the file content already includes the change, but you can get conflicts or redundant commits in history. Git has some heuristics: - “patch-id” can help detect equivalent patches in some contexts (e.g., rebase uses it sometimes), but it’s not a universal deduplication guarantee. The safest way to avoid duplicates is: - Prefer merge/rebase to move a coherent series of commits - Use cherry-pick sparingly for isolated commits where duplication is acceptable or where you won’t later merge the original branch
Can we go deeper into how Git decides the "fork point" for rebase?

Git typically uses the merge base between the branch you’re rebasing and the upstream branch. If you run: git rebase main while on feature, Git finds: merge-base(feature, main) Call it B. Then it takes commits reachable from feature but not from B, and replays them onto main. However, there’s an additional nuance when your branch has tracked upstream and you’ve previously rebased/merged: “fork-point” logic. git rebase can use --fork-point to find a better starting point when the upstream branch has been rebased or when the branch tips moved in ways that make the merge-base not represent the original fork. It uses the reflog of the upstream-tracking branch to guess where you branched off. You might see: git rebase --fork-point origin/main This is useful if origin/main moved (e.g., history rewrite) and merge-base becomes misleading. In typical everyday cases where upstream history is stable, merge-base is fine.
So if main never rewrites history, I probably don't need fork-point?

Right. If the upstream branch you’re rebasing onto is stable (no force pushes, no rebases), the merge-base is usually the correct fork point and --fork-point doesn’t add much. fork-point is mainly a “recover from upstream rewrite” helper or for cases where your local notion of upstream moved and merge-base would cause you to replay commits you already effectively have.
What's the difference between "git merge --no-ff" and just doing a normal merge?

Normal merge behavior: - If a fast-forward is possible, Git will fast-forward by default (no merge commit). - If not possible, Git creates a merge commit. git merge --no-ff forces a merge commit even when a fast-forward is possible. Example: main: A - B feature: A - B - C - D Normal merge on main: - would fast-forward main to D, no merge commit. With --no-ff: - Git creates merge commit M with parents B and D: A - B - C - D - M \       / (conceptually, but since feature is linear, it looks like an extra commit) Why do it? - To preserve a visible “integration point” marking that C and D came in as a unit (a feature boundary). - It can make reverting a whole feature easier: revert the merge commit instead of a range of commits (though that has its own caveats). If you prefer explicit boundaries, --no-ff is useful. If you prefer purely linear history, avoid it.
Reverting a merge commit seems tricky. Why is it tricky?

Because a merge commit has two (or more) parents, and “reverting” it is ambiguous: which parent is the mainline you want to keep? When you revert a normal commit, Git can compute the inverse patch relative to its parent. With a merge commit, there isn’t a single parent. So you do: git revert -m 1 <merge_sha> The -m 1 says: treat parent #1 as the mainline, and revert the changes introduced by merging the other parent(s) into it. If you pick the wrong parent number, you can revert the wrong direction. Also, reverting a merge commit doesn’t “undo history”; it creates a new commit that undoes the net changes. Later merges can get complicated because Git sees that the merge happened (in history) even though you reverted its content.
How does "git rebase" decide what to do with my local uncommitted changes? Do I need to stash every time?

By default, Git requires a clean working directory to start a rebase (no unstaged or staged but uncommitted changes), because it needs to manipulate HEAD and apply commits cleanly. If you have uncommitted changes, Git will refuse: - “cannot rebase: You have unstaged changes” etc. Options: 1) Commit your changes (even as a temporary commit you might later squash/fixup). 2) Stash: git stash push -m "wip" git rebase ... git stash pop 3) Use autostash: git rebase --autostash ... or set rebase.autoStash true. So you don’t always need to stash manually, but you need either a clean tree or autostash.
Can you explain the relationship between HEAD, branch pointers, and rebase/merge? I think that's where I get lost.

This is foundational, and once it clicks, rebase vs merge becomes much less mysterious. Key objects: - Commits: immutable snapshots with parent pointers - Branch: just a movable pointer (ref) to a commit - HEAD: usually points to the current branch ref (symbolic ref), not directly to a commit (unless detached HEAD) Example: If you’re on feature, HEAD -> refs/heads/feature -> commit D. When you commit: - Git creates a new commit whose parent is the current commit. - It moves the current branch pointer (feature) to the new commit. - HEAD still points to feature. Merge: - When you merge main into feature, Git creates a new merge commit M with parents (feature tip, main tip). - Then it moves feature pointer to M. Rebase: - When you rebase feature onto main, Git: 1) identifies commits on feature not in main (C, D) 2) checks out main (or rather, uses main as base) 3) creates new commits C', D' on top of main tip 4) moves feature pointer to D' So rebase doesn’t “move commits”; it creates new commits and moves the branch pointer. This “branch pointers move; commits don’t” model is the key.
Detached HEAD happens to me sometimes when I checkout a commit. Is that bad?

Detached HEAD means HEAD points directly to a commit, not to a branch ref. It’s not inherently bad; it’s just a state where new commits won’t be on any named branch unless you create one. Example: git checkout <sha> Now HEAD is detached at that commit. If you commit now: - A new commit is created, but no branch pointer advances (because you’re not on a branch). - The commit is reachable only via HEAD/reflog until it’s garbage-collected (it can be recovered via reflog, but it’s easy to lose track of). If you want to keep work from detached HEAD: git switch -c new-branch-name This creates a branch pointing to the current commit and attaches HEAD to it. Detached HEAD is useful for: - Inspecting old versions - Building/testing at a particular commit - Temporary experiments Just remember to create a branch if you want to keep commits.
Switching topics slightly: what's the difference between "git switch" and "git checkout"? People tell me to use switch now.

git checkout historically did two different jobs: 1) Switch branches / detach HEAD 2) Restore files from another commit to the working tree That made it powerful but confusing. Git introduced: - git switch: for switching branches (and creating branches) - git restore: for restoring files Examples: - git checkout feature  ==  git switch feature - git checkout -b feature  ==  git switch -c feature - git checkout -- file.txt  ==  git restore file.txt - git checkout <commit> -- file.txt  ==  git restore --source <commit> file.txt So switch/restore are “more intention-revealing.” checkout still works and is ubiquitous, but switch/restore can reduce mistakes.
Back to rebase: what's the difference between "git rebase main" and "git rebase origin/main"?

It’s about what “main” points to. - main is your local branch ref. It might be up-to-date, or it might be behind if you haven’t fetched/pulled. - origin/main is a remote-tracking ref updated by git fetch. It reflects the last fetched state of the remote’s main. So: git rebase origin/main rebases onto the last fetched remote state. git rebase main rebases onto your local main state. If you keep local main updated, they’re equivalent. If not, origin/main is often the safer choice after a fetch.
I've run into conflicts during rebase and sometimes I forget what I was doing. How do I inspect the state mid-rebase?

When a rebase is in progress, Git stores metadata under .git/rebase-apply or .git/rebase-merge (depending on rebase backend). You don’t usually need to look there directly, but you can use commands: - git status This will tell you: - “rebase in progress” - which commit it’s applying - what to do next (continue/abort/skip) - git rebase --show-current-patch Shows the patch for the commit currently being applied (helpful for understanding what you’re trying to achieve). - git log --oneline --decorate --graph --all Shows where HEAD is (often detached during rebase) and where your branch refs are. - cat .git/rebase-merge/git-rebase-todo Shows remaining commits to apply (if using that backend). Most of the time, git status is the best “what’s happening?” tool.
Why is HEAD detached during rebase?

During rebase, Git is effectively checking out the new base and then applying commits. It needs to move HEAD through intermediate states as it creates new commits. Rather than moving your branch ref step-by-step (which could leave it in an inconsistent state if something fails), Git often operates with HEAD detached and only updates the branch ref at the end (or at safe points). This is implementation detail-ish, but it explains why git status might show: “HEAD detached at <sha>” during a rebase. You still end up with your branch updated when rebase completes.
Sometimes after a rebase, my branch has commits that look duplicated. Like I see the same message twice. What's going on?

Common causes: 1) You rebased commits that were already present upstream (or equivalents) If your fork point was wrong, rebase might replay commits that are already on main (or that were cherry-picked into main). Because Git compares commit IDs, not just diffs, it can replay “duplicate changes” if it doesn’t realize they’re equivalent. 2) You merged main into feature earlier, then rebased If feature contains merge commits from main, rebasing can get messy unless you use --rebase-merges or carefully choose the range. You might end up replaying commits that came from main as well as your own. 3) You cherry-picked something and then rebased Cherry-picks create new SHAs. If later you rebase a branch that contains both original and cherry-picked versions of similar changes, you can see duplicates. How to diagnose: - Compare ranges: git log --oneline main..feature - Look for duplicate patch content: git show <sha> or git range-diff git range-diff is particularly good: git range-diff main...feature@{1} main...feature It compares two versions of a commit series (before/after rebase) and highlights what changed.
I've never used range-diff. Can you explain it more?

git range-diff compares two commit ranges as sequences of patches, showing how one series differs from another. It’s like a “diff of diffs” for commit series. Typical use: after rebasing or reordering commits, you want to confirm you didn’t accidentally change the content. Example: You had feature before rebase: old-feature After rebase: feature You can compare the series relative to main: git range-diff main..old-feature main..feature Or if you used reflog: git range-diff main..feature@{1} main..feature (where feature@{1} is the branch tip before the last operation) Output shows which commits correspond, which were dropped, which were added, and how patches changed. It’s extremely useful when doing interactive rebase or when preparing a clean series and wanting confidence you didn’t alter semantics.
Nice. Another thing: is there any reason to avoid rebase because it "lies" about history?

That’s a philosophical point, but there’s a technical angle. Rebase changes the recorded ancestry. Instead of “I branched at B and did C and D while main did E and F,” rebased history looks like “main did E and F, then I did C and D.” It doesn’t show that the work was done in parallel. If you care about preserving the true chronology and integration points, merge commits keep that information. But rebase isn’t necessarily “lying” if your goal is to present a clean, logical progression of changes rather than a timeline of development. Many people treat commit history as a curated narrative, not a raw activity log. So the reason to avoid rebase is not moral; it’s practical: - Don’t rewrite shared history - Don’t lose meaningful merge structure if it matters - Don’t complicate traceability if you rely on exact branch topology If those don’t apply, rebase can be a good tool.
How does GitHub/GitLab style "rebase and merge" differ from me doing rebase locally?

Ignoring any specific platform, the general idea of “rebase and merge” as an integration strategy is: - Take the commits from the feature branch - Rebase them onto the current tip of main - Then fast-forward main to include them (no merge commit) If you do it locally, you: 1) git fetch origin 2) git checkout feature 3) git rebase origin/main 4) git checkout main 5) git merge --ff-only feature 6) git push origin main The platform button typically automates something like that on the server side. Important differences: - The server can ensure it’s rebased onto the latest main at the time of integration. - It can avoid needing you to force-push your feature branch (because it can create the rebased commits as part of the merge operation without rewriting your branch ref, depending on implementation). - It may preserve authorship and committer metadata in a specific way. But conceptually, it’s “linear integration by replaying commits.”
If I do rebase locally and force push, is there a safe way to do it without accidentally losing commits?

Yes. A few safety habits: 1) Use --force-with-lease, not --force git push --force-with-lease origin feature This prevents overwriting remote changes you didn’t have locally. 2) Create a backup branch before big rewrites git branch backup/feature-before-rewrite Then rebase. If something goes wrong, you can find the old tip easily. 3) Use reflog If you lose something, git reflog often recovers it. 4) Inspect what will change - git log --oneline --decorate --graph origin/feature..feature Shows what you’re about to push relative to remote. - git log --oneline --decorate --graph feature..origin/feature Shows what remote has that you don’t (danger sign if you’re about to force push). 5) Keep your working tree clean Avoid mixing uncommitted changes with history rewrite operations.
What's the difference between "git reset --soft" and "git reset --hard"? I see these recommended during cleanup.

git reset moves the current branch pointer (and HEAD) to another commit, and it optionally updates the index (staging area) and working tree. Modes: - --soft: move HEAD/branch only; keep index and working tree as-is. Effect: commits are “uncommitted” but changes remain staged. Use: combine commits, redo commit message, etc. - --mixed (default): move HEAD/branch and reset index to match; working tree unchanged. Effect: changes become unstaged in working tree. - --hard: move HEAD/branch, reset index, and reset working tree to match target commit. Effect: discards local changes (dangerous). In cleanup workflows: - After an interactive rebase, you might use reset --soft to regroup commits. - Use hard only when you’re sure you don’t need local changes (or you have them stashed).
Is reset related to revert?

They’re different tools: - reset: moves branch pointer; can rewrite history (especially if commits were already shared). It changes what the branch “is.” - revert: creates a new commit that undoes changes; doesn’t rewrite history. It changes the content while preserving the commit history. Use reset when: - You’re correcting local history before sharing. - You want to move HEAD back and redo commits. Use revert when: - The commit is already in a shared history, and you want a safe undo.
Can you give me a recommended "mental workflow" for my case: feature branches, sometimes pulling main updates, then integrating?

One coherent mental workflow: While working on feature: 1) Make commits as you go (try to keep them logical if possible). 2) Periodically incorporate main updates by rebasing: - git fetch origin - git rebase origin/main Resolve conflicts if needed. 3) If you already pushed feature and need to update it after rebasing: - git push --force-with-lease Before integrating into main: 4) Interactive rebase to clean up: - git rebase -i origin/main Squash/fixup/reword to produce a clean commit series. 5) Rebase once more onto latest origin/main (in case it moved): - git fetch origin - git rebase origin/main 6) Integrate by fast-forwarding main (or by a linear merge method): - git checkout main - git merge --ff-only feature Alternative if you don’t want force pushes: - While working: merge origin/main into feature instead of rebasing. - At the end: merge feature into main. History will have merge commits but no rewriting. The choice is: linear history with occasional force pushes vs non-rewriting history with merge commits.
If I choose the rebase approach, should I set pull.rebase = true?

It can be helpful, but only if you understand what it will do in each branch you pull on. Setting: git config --global pull.rebase true makes git pull use rebase instead of merge by default. Pros: - Prevents accidental merge commits when pulling. - Keeps branches linear if you regularly pull updates. Cons: - If you pull on a branch that is already “published” and you don’t intend to rewrite, it can surprise you (though pull --rebase typically rebases local commits on top of fetched commits; if you have no local-only commits, it just fast-forwards). - If you have local commits and you pull frequently, you’ll rewrite them often (fine if that’s intended). Safer variant: git config --global pull.ff only This makes pull fail if it would create a merge commit. You can then choose explicitly to merge or rebase. Many people like this because it forces explicitness. You can also set per-branch behavior: git config branch.feature.rebase true
What's the difference between pull.ff=only and merge.ff=only?

They apply to different commands: - pull.ff=only affects git pull behavior when it would need to integrate remote changes. It ensures pull will only fast-forward; if it can’t, it fails instead of merging. - merge.ff=only affects git merge behavior, requiring that merges be fast-forward only (no merge commits). If a fast-forward isn’t possible, merge fails. If your goal is “never create merge commits accidentally,” setting pull.ff=only helps for pulls. Setting merge.ff=only helps for merges. But you might still want to allow merge commits sometimes, so choose carefully.
Let's say I'm on feature and I run "git pull" without thinking. What determines whether it merges or rebases?

It depends on configuration and flags, in this precedence order: 1) Command-line flags: - git pull --rebase (explicit rebase) - git pull --no-rebase (explicit merge behavior) - git pull --ff-only (only fast-forward) 2) Branch-specific config: - branch.<name>.rebase 3) Global pull config: - pull.rebase - pull.ff If none is set, default is to merge (and create a merge commit if needed). Also, git pull integrates from the configured upstream branch (tracking branch), usually origin/<branchname> unless configured otherwise. You can always see upstream with: git branch -vv
I think I understand. Can you give a final summary of "rebase vs merge" in one paragraph, but technical?

Merge creates a new commit that joins two histories without rewriting existing commits, preserving the true branch topology and keeping commit IDs stable, which is safest for shared/published history; rebase rewrites a branch by replaying its commits onto a new base (creating new commit IDs) to produce a cleaner, typically linear history and to make later integration fast-forwardable, which is ideal for local/unpublished commit series and for curating commit structure via interactive rebase, but it requires force-with-lease when updating a remote branch whose history you rewrote and should be avoided on histories that others or other processes depend on.
Great. One last thing: can you propose a small practice exercise I can do in a fresh repo to see all of this?

Initialize a new repo and intentionally create divergence, then try both merge and rebase. Exercise: 1) Setup - mkdir git-practice && cd git-practice - git init - Create file.txt with “line1” - git add file.txt && git commit -m "A" - Append “line2” and commit -m "B" Now main has A-B. 2) Create feature - git switch -c feature - Append “feature1” and commit -m "C" - Append “feature2” and commit -m "D" 3) Advance main - git switch main - Append “main1” and commit -m "E" - Append “main2” and commit -m "F" 4) Visualize - git log --graph --oneline --decorate --all 5) Try merge main into feature - git switch feature - git merge main - Inspect graph again 6) Reset back before merge (use reflog) - git reflog (find pre-merge HEAD) - git reset --hard <that-sha> 7) Try rebase feature onto main - git rebase main - Inspect graph 8) Create a conflict - On main, edit the same line in file.txt and commit - On feature, edit that same line in a commit - Rebase feature onto main and resolve conflict - Observe how rebase stops per commit and uses rebase --continue Throughout, keep running: git log --graph --oneline --decorate --all and git status to build intuition. If you want, tell me what OS/shell you use and I can write the exact commands with file edits that reliably cause a conflict.

I’ve got a slow SQL query in Postgres. It’s a JOIN across a few tables and takes ~8 seconds on ~2M rows. I’m trying to optimize it with indexing. Here’s the query and EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS). Can you help interpret it and propose improvements?

Paste the query, the schemas (columns + types + PK/FK), row counts, and the EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS). Also mention Postgres version, whether tables are partitioned, and any existing indexes. With that I can suggest concrete index changes and query rewrites.

Main issues visible: 1) Seq Scan on order_items (9.4M) feeding a hash join. Even though you have order_items(order_id) index, planner chose seq scan because it expects to join many rows anyway. But you’re joining order_items to a filtered subset of orders (last 90 days + status). Ideally you want to start from filtered orders, then fetch only matching order_items via index (nested loop) or via a join that can leverage an index. 2) HashAggregate is spilling (Batches: 5, Disk Usage 20GB? actually 20480kB = 20MB). Still, spill indicates work_mem might be low for this query. 3) refunds is seq scanned; 120k is small, but join multiplicity: because you join refunds to orders and also join order_items, you might be multiplying refund amounts by number of items per order unless aggregation is carefully done. In your query, SUM(r.amount) across joined rows will duplicate refund rows per item row. That can both inflate results and add work. First: correctness: with JOIN order_items and LEFT JOIN refunds on orders, each refund row repeats per order_item row, so SUM(r.amount) will be multiplied by count(order_items) per order. Unless there’s at most one item per order, results are wrong. Same for COUNT(DISTINCT o.id) is okay (distinct), but SUM(r.amount) is not distinct. So you probably need to pre-aggregate refunds per order, and maybe items per order, then join those aggregates to orders/customers. Rewrite approach: - Filter orders first (created_at + status). - Aggregate order_items per order_id: sum(qty*price) gross_per_order. - Aggregate refunds per order_id: sum(amount) refund_per_order. - Then group by customer. Example: WITH filtered_orders AS ( SELECT id, customer_id FROM orders WHERE created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' AND status IN ('paid','shipped') ), items AS ( SELECT oi.order_id, SUM(oi.qty*oi.price) AS gross FROM order_items oi JOIN filtered_orders fo ON fo.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id ), ref AS ( SELECT r.order_id, SUM(r.amount) AS refunded FROM refunds r JOIN filtered_orders fo ON fo.id = r.order_id GROUP BY r.order_id ) SELECT c.id, COUNT(fo.id) AS order_count, SUM(items.gross) AS gross, COALESCE(SUM(ref.refunded),0) AS refunded FROM customers c JOIN filtered_orders fo ON fo.customer_id = c.id JOIN items ON items.order_id = fo.id LEFT JOIN ref ON ref.order_id = fo.id WHERE c.status='active' GROUP BY c.id ORDER BY gross DESC LIMIT 50; Now indexing: - On orders, you want an index that supports the filter and join: (created_at) alone isn’t enough. Consider a multicolumn index on (created_at, status, customer_id, id) or (status, created_at, customer_id) depending on selectivity. Since created_at is range, put it first often works; but status filter is IN with small set, might be selective too. A good compromise: CREATE INDEX orders_created_status_cust_id_idx ON orders (created_at, status, customer_id, id); Or (status, created_at, customer_id, id) if status is more selective. - On customers, since you filter by status and group by id, index on (status, id) could avoid seq scan: CREATE INDEX customers_status_id_idx ON customers (status, id); - On order_items, you already have (order_id). For aggregation, (order_id) is enough; but if you can do index-only scans, include qty, price: CREATE INDEX order_items_order_id_inc_idx ON order_items (order_id) INCLUDE (qty, price); - On refunds, similarly: CREATE INDEX refunds_order_id_inc_idx ON refunds (order_id) INCLUDE (amount); Also consider increasing work_mem for this query to avoid hash aggregate spill (e.g. SET work_mem='256MB' for session) but first fix join multiplicity and plan shape. Can you confirm whether refunds can have multiple rows per order and whether order_items has multiple rows per order (likely yes)? If yes, the rewrite is both correctness and performance.

Good catch—yes, multiple items per order and multiple refunds per order sometimes. So current query is wrong. I’ll rewrite with pre-aggregation. But I’d like to understand why the planner did a seq scan on order_items even though it could use order_items(order_id). Is it because it expects most orders match the 90-day filter?

Exactly. The planner estimates cardinalities and cost. In your plan it expects 220k orders after filter, but then joins to 9.4M items. If average items per order is, say, 4–5, then items for those orders might be ~880k–1.1M, which is far less than 9.4M, so an index-driven access could be good. But the planner didn’t choose it, which suggests either: - It believes a large fraction of order_items will match (maybe because it thinks most orders are in last 90 days, or correlation stats are off). - Random I/O cost assumptions: many index lookups can be costlier than a sequential scan if it expects lots of hits. - It can’t easily start from filtered orders and then nested loop into items because the join order chosen was different (it scanned order_items first, then hashed orders). Fixes: 1) Rewrite with CTE/subqueries that encourage starting from filtered orders (though PG can inline CTEs since 12; still, the rewritten structure changes join graph and may help). 2) Better indexes and statistics so the planner sees selectivity. 3) Extended statistics: create stats on orders(status, created_at) to help estimate combined selectivity: CREATE STATISTICS orders_status_created_at_stats ON status, created_at FROM orders; ANALYZE orders; Also check column stats target for created_at if distribution is skewed: ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN created_at SET STATISTICS 1000; ANALYZE orders; And consider lowering random_page_cost if you’re on SSD and it’s too high (default 4). Many people set to 1.1–1.5. That can make index plans more attractive.

I implemented the rewrite using derived tables (not CTE). Here’s the new query: SELECT c.id, COUNT(o.id) AS order_count, SUM(oi.gross) AS gross, COALESCE(SUM(r.refunded),0) AS refunded FROM customers c JOIN ( SELECT id, customer_id FROM orders WHERE created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' AND status IN ('paid','shipped') ) o ON o.customer_id = c.id JOIN ( SELECT order_id, SUM(qty*price) AS gross FROM order_items GROUP BY order_id ) oi ON oi.order_id = o.id LEFT JOIN ( SELECT order_id, SUM(amount) AS refunded FROM refunds GROUP BY order_id ) r ON r.order_id = o.id WHERE c.status='active' GROUP BY c.id ORDER BY gross DESC LIMIT 50; It’s still slow (~6.5s). EXPLAIN shows it’s aggregating ALL order_items and ALL refunds before joining to filtered orders. How do I force it to aggregate only relevant order_ids?

Push the filter into the aggregation inputs by joining to filtered orders inside the subquery, so the GROUP BY only touches matching rows. Key change: in the oi subquery, join order_items to the filtered orders set (o) before grouping. Same for refunds. But you can’t reference the outer derived table alias directly unless you use a CTE or repeat the filtered orders subquery. Two clean patterns: Pattern A: CTE for filtered_orders (PG14 inlines by default, so not an optimization fence unless MATERIALIZED used): WITH filtered_orders AS ( SELECT id, customer_id FROM orders WHERE created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' AND status IN ('paid','shipped') ) , items AS ( SELECT oi.order_id, SUM(oi.qty*oi.price) AS gross FROM order_items oi JOIN filtered_orders fo ON fo.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id ) , ref AS ( SELECT r.order_id, SUM(r.amount) AS refunded FROM refunds r JOIN filtered_orders fo ON fo.id = r.order_id GROUP BY r.order_id ) SELECT c.id, COUNT(fo.id) AS order_count, SUM(items.gross) AS gross, COALESCE(SUM(ref.refunded),0) AS refunded FROM customers c JOIN filtered_orders fo ON fo.customer_id = c.id JOIN items ON items.order_id = fo.id LEFT JOIN ref ON ref.order_id = fo.id WHERE c.status='active' GROUP BY c.id ORDER BY gross DESC LIMIT 50; Pattern B: LATERAL aggregates per order (can be great if filtered_orders is small and indexes are good): WITH filtered_orders AS (...) SELECT c.id, COUNT(fo.id) AS order_count, SUM(oi.gross) AS gross, COALESCE(SUM(r.refunded),0) AS refunded FROM customers c JOIN filtered_orders fo ON fo.customer_id = c.id JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(qty*price) AS gross FROM order_items WHERE order_id = fo.id ) oi ON true LEFT JOIN LATERAL ( SELECT SUM(amount) AS refunded FROM refunds WHERE order_id = fo.id ) r ON true WHERE c.status='active' GROUP BY c.id ORDER BY gross DESC LIMIT 50; Pattern B uses many small index lookups; good when you have order_items(order_id) and refunds(order_id) and filtered orders count isn’t huge. Given you have ~220k orders in 90 days, Pattern B might still be heavy (220k index probes into order_items), but could be okay if cached and SSD. Pattern A does a single pass over matching order_items via a hash/semi join. Also: add the INCLUDE indexes I mentioned so aggregation can be index-only.

I tried Pattern A with CTE and added: CREATE INDEX order_items_order_id_inc_idx ON order_items(order_id) INCLUDE (qty, price); CREATE INDEX refunds_order_id_inc_idx ON refunds(order_id) INCLUDE (amount); CREATE INDEX customers_status_id_idx ON customers(status, id); CREATE INDEX orders_created_status_cust_id_idx ON orders(created_at, status, customer_id, id); After ANALYZE, runtime is ~2.4s. Better, but still not great. EXPLAIN shows a Hash Join between filtered_orders and order_items, but it still scans a lot of order_items. It’s doing an Index Only Scan on order_items_order_id_inc_idx but says “Heap Fetches: 2,000,000”. Why so many heap fetches if it’s index-only?

Index-only scan still needs heap fetches when the visibility map indicates tuples might not be all-visible. Heap fetches happen to confirm tuple visibility for MVCC. High heap fetches typically mean the table has lots of recently modified pages not marked all-visible. Ways to reduce heap fetches: - Run VACUUM (or ensure autovacuum is keeping up) so pages become all-visible. - If the table is heavily updated/inserted, index-only scans may never be fully heap-free; but for append-mostly tables, vacuum helps a lot. - Check autovacuum settings and table stats: SELECT relname, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE relname IN ('order_items','orders','refunds','customers'); Also check: SELECT * FROM pgstattuple('order_items'); (if extension installed) or pg_stat_all_tables. Additionally, the plan scanning many order_items could still be because 90-day orders correspond to a big fraction of all items. If your business has most orders in last 90 days, then scanning most items is unavoidable unless you partition by time or keep a summary table. Question: what fraction of orders are in last 90 days? EXPLAIN earlier showed bitmap index scan returning 650k candidate rows, then filtered to 220k after status. Out of 2.1M total, 650k is ~31%. That might imply ~30% of items too (2.8M). 2–3M item rows aggregated might plausibly be 2.4s.

Makes sense. Autovacuum might be lagging; order_items is insert-heavy. Another angle: Would partitioning orders/order_items by created_at help? order_items doesn’t have created_at though; only orders does.

Partitioning can help if you can prune partitions early. Since filter is on orders.created_at, partitioning orders by created_at would prune old orders. But order_items joins by order_id, not created_at, so it won’t prune order_items unless: - order_items also carries created_at (copied from order) and is partitioned similarly, or - you partition order_items by order_id ranges aligned with orders partitions (hard), or - you use declarative partitioning with foreign keys and include order_created_at in order_items. Common approach: add order_created_at to order_items (denormalized) maintained by application or trigger, then partition order_items by that date. Then query can filter items by order_created_at >= now()-90d without joining first, enabling pruning. But it’s a schema change and data duplication. Alternative: maintain summary tables: - order_totals(order_id, gross, refunded, updated_at) - customer_90day_totals(customer_id, gross, refunded, order_count) refreshed periodically or incrementally. If this query is frequent and latency-sensitive, summaries beat on-the-fly aggregation.
I can’t change schema drastically right now. Can we squeeze more out of query/index tuning? For example, could we use partial indexes on orders for statuses paid/shipped?

Yes, partial indexes can reduce index size and improve scans when the predicate matches exactly. Example: CREATE INDEX orders_recent_paid_shipped_idx ON orders (created_at, customer_id, id) WHERE status IN ('paid','shipped'); But note: your predicate includes created_at >= now()-interval '90 days' which is non-immutable; you can’t put “recent 90 days” in partial index predicate reliably. But you can partial on status only. That still helps because it avoids indexing other statuses. Also consider index ordering: if you mostly filter by status then date, try: CREATE INDEX orders_status_created_cust_id_idx ON orders (status, created_at, customer_id, id); Then the planner can do an index range scan per status value. Also: If you only need id and customer_id from filtered orders, an index that covers them helps index-only: ON orders(status, created_at) INCLUDE (id, customer_id) But since id is PK, including it is redundant? Not exactly—PK index is separate. INCLUDE makes this index cover required columns, enabling index-only scan on this index. So: CREATE INDEX orders_status_created_inc_idx ON orders (status, created_at) INCLUDE (id, customer_id); Then filtered_orders CTE can be satisfied from this index alone.

I created orders_status_created_inc_idx and dropped the older composite index. Now filtered_orders uses index-only scan with few heap fetches. Total runtime ~1.8s. Still, the aggregation on items dominates. Any tricks like using incremental sort or top-N optimization to avoid aggregating all customers if we only need top 50 by gross?

Unfortunately, to know the top 50 customers by gross, the database generally must compute gross per customer for all customers in scope (or at least enough to be sure). There isn’t a generic way to stop early because gross is an aggregate over many rows. However, you can sometimes reduce work by: - Aggregating per order first (items per order), then per customer. That’s already what we’re doing. It can reduce row volume if many items per order. - Ensuring join order: items aggregate keyed by order_id, then join to filtered_orders, then group by customer_id. That keeps intermediate rows smaller. - Using parallelism: allow parallel aggregate and scans. Check if plan uses workers. If not, maybe max_parallel_workers_per_gather too low or parallel_cost settings too high. Try: SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 4; SET parallel_setup_cost = 0; SET parallel_tuple_cost = 0.01; (Testing only.) Also ensure order_items table is large enough for parallelism and not blocked by functions.
Plan shows “Workers Planned: 2, Workers Launched: 2” on order_items index-only scan. HashAggregate still single-threaded though. Is there a way to make the GROUP BY parallel?

Postgres can do parallel aggregates, but not in all cases. HashAggregate can be parallelized as Partial HashAggregate + Gather + Finalize Aggregate if conditions allow. If you’re seeing a single HashAggregate, it might be because: - work_mem too low causing spill; parallel hash aggregate might be disabled or less likely. - The query shape (CTEs/subqueries) results in non-parallel-safe nodes. - enable_parallel_hash might be off (it’s on by default in newer versions), but check: SHOW enable_parallel_hash; Also check: SHOW max_parallel_workers; SHOW max_parallel_workers_per_gather; SHOW parallel_leader_participation; If you use a subquery that materializes, it might block parallelism. Ensure you didn’t mark CTE as MATERIALIZED. In PG14, CTEs inline; but if planner decides to materialize, it can affect. You can experiment with enabling/disabling: SET enable_hashagg = on; SET enable_sort = on; And see if it chooses GroupAggregate with sort, which sometimes parallelizes differently (but may require sorting huge sets).

enable_parallel_hash is on. work_mem is 4MB default. If I bump work_mem to 128MB for this session, runtime drops to 1.2s. HashAggregate no longer spills. Is it safe to just increase work_mem globally?

Don’t raise it globally to a high value without considering concurrency: work_mem is per operation per query, and a complex query can use multiple work_mem allocations. If many queries run concurrently, you can exhaust RAM. Safer approaches: - Set work_mem per role or per session for the reporting workload. - Use statement_timeout + resource groups externally. - In app, issue “SET LOCAL work_mem='128MB'” inside the transaction for this query only. Also consider maintenance_work_mem for vacuum/build indexes, separate.
Understood. Another question: would rewriting aggregation to use COUNT(*) FILTER or SUM() FILTER help? Or using DISTINCT aggregates?

FILTER won’t change performance meaningfully here; it’s mostly syntactic. DISTINCT aggregates are expensive; you already removed the need by restructuring. The big win came from correct join cardinality and avoiding spill.
Can we validate the final query structure you’d recommend, given all this?

Recommended query (keeping it clear and planner-friendly): WITH filtered_orders AS ( SELECT id, customer_id FROM orders WHERE status IN ('paid','shipped') AND created_at >= now() - interval '90 days' ), items AS ( SELECT oi.order_id, SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS gross FROM order_items oi JOIN filtered_orders fo ON fo.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id ), ref AS ( SELECT r.order_id, SUM(r.amount) AS refunded FROM refunds r JOIN filtered_orders fo ON fo.id = r.order_id GROUP BY r.order_id ) SELECT c.id, COUNT(fo.id) AS order_count, SUM(items.gross) AS gross, COALESCE(SUM(ref.refunded), 0) AS refunded FROM filtered_orders fo JOIN customers c ON c.id = fo.customer_id JOIN items ON items.order_id = fo.id LEFT JOIN ref ON ref.order_id = fo.id WHERE c.status = 'active' GROUP BY c.id ORDER BY gross DESC LIMIT 50; Note: join customers after filtered_orders; either way is fine, but this can reduce customers considered earlier. Indexes: - customers(status, id) - orders(status, created_at) INCLUDE (id, customer_id) - order_items(order_id) INCLUDE (qty, price) - refunds(order_id) INCLUDE (amount) Operational: - Ensure autovacuum keeps visibility map updated for index-only scans. - Use SET LOCAL work_mem for this query to avoid spill. - Keep statistics up to date; consider extended stats on orders(status, created_at).
If I want to avoid the CTEs entirely, will the planner behave similarly?

You can use subqueries; but you must ensure the filtered_orders subquery is referenced in items/ref subqueries so they can restrict input. Without that, they’ll aggregate whole tables. A CTE is the cleanest way to share filtered_orders. In PG14 it should inline, so performance is usually fine. If you remove CTEs, you’ll end up repeating the filtered_orders subquery multiple times, which can be worse or lead to different plans.
Last thing: any quick checklist to confirm I’m not missing something obvious in the plan?

Checklist: - No unintended row multiplication (verify joins before aggregates). - Aggregates not spilling (check “Disk Usage” / “Batches”). - Index-only scans have low heap fetches (or acceptable given write rate). - Filter selectivity estimates reasonable (estimated vs actual rows). - Parallel workers used on big scans if beneficial. - Buffers: excessive reads indicate cache misses; consider shared_buffers or OS cache, but that’s system-level. If you paste the new EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) with work_mem 4MB vs 128MB, we can pinpoint remaining hotspots.

Here’s the EXPLAIN with work_mem=128MB (session local). Execution ~1.18s. Limit -> Sort top-N -> HashAggregate (no spill) -> Hash Join (items to filtered_orders) -> GroupAggregate on order_items (actually it’s HashAggregate per order_id) -> filtered_orders index-only scan Then join to customers and left join refunds aggregate similarly. Heap fetches: order_items 350k, orders 10k.

That looks close to optimal without schema changes. Remaining 350k heap fetches is fine for insert-heavy table. If you want to reduce further, ensure autovacuum is aggressive enough on order_items: - lower autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor for that table - consider autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor (PG13+) - increase autovacuum_work_mem, autovacuum_max_workers if needed Per-table settings: ALTER TABLE order_items SET (autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.02); ALTER TABLE order_items SET (autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.02); Tune based on write volume.
Thanks. That’s all I needed.

Actually one more: we run this report for different time windows (7, 30, 90, 365 days). Would it help to parameterize the interval, or does “now() - interval …” hurt plan caching? We use prepared statements.

With prepared statements, the main issue isn’t plan caching per se, it’s that Postgres may choose a “generic plan” after a few executions, and that generic plan might be suboptimal for very different time windows. Two key points: 1) now() is STABLE, not IMMUTABLE - It’s fine in a query; it won’t prevent index use. - But the exact cutoff changes each execution, which is normal. That doesn’t inherently “break” caching. 2) Prepared statements: generic vs custom plan - If you prepare a statement like: created_at >= now() - $1::interval then the selectivity can vary wildly (7 days vs 365 days). - Postgres may decide to reuse a generic plan that’s “okay on average” but not best for extremes. What to do: - If you use psql or can set it per session, test: SET plan_cache_mode = force_custom_plan; and compare runtimes across windows. If custom plans are much better, you’ve found the issue. - In application code, you can avoid server-side prepared statements for this report (or use a separate connection / role setting). - If you’re using a driver that aggressively prepares (JDBC, etc.), you can sometimes disable prepare for that statement, or set the driver’s prepare threshold so it doesn’t switch to server-side prepare. Also: consider passing a concrete timestamp instead of an interval: - Compute cutoff in app: cutoff_ts = now_utc - days - Query uses: created_at >= $1::timestamptz This often gives more stable selectivity estimation than an interval parameter, and can make custom planning more predictable. If you want, tell me your driver (JDBC/psycopg/etc.) and whether you’re using server-side prepares explicitly; I can suggest the least invasive way to force custom plans just for this report.
We use Go with pgx. It uses prepared statements by default. How do I avoid generic plans just for this one query?

In pgx, you can control statement caching / preparing at a couple levels: - If you’re using pgxpool and the default query execution mode, pgx will often use the extended protocol and may prepare/describe statements depending on config. Practical options (pick one): 1) Use “simple protocol” for this query (no prepared statement) - pgx has QueryExecModeSimpleProtocol (in pgx v5). - You can set it per query via context if you’re using ConnConfig.DefaultQueryExecMode, or create a dedicated connection/pool for reporting. Conceptually: - Keep your normal pool as-is. - For this report query, run it on a connection configured with simple protocol so Postgres plans each time (custom plan). 2) Disable statement cache for this query - If you’re using pgx’s statement cache, you can set it to 0 for the connection used by this report, or use a separate pool with statement cache disabled. 3) Force custom plans at the server for that session/transaction - Execute before the query on that connection: SET LOCAL plan_cache_mode = force_custom_plan; This only applies within the current transaction. So you’d do: BEGIN; SET LOCAL plan_cache_mode = force_custom_plan; <run report query> COMMIT; This is often the simplest “surgical” fix if you can afford wrapping the report query in a transaction (usually fine). Also, as mentioned: pass a timestamp cutoff parameter rather than an interval parameter; that alone can reduce variance. If you tell me whether you already wrap this in a transaction and your pgx major version, I can suggest the cleanest way to implement option (1) or (3).
We’re on pgx v5. We can wrap in a transaction. If we do SET LOCAL plan_cache_mode, will it affect other queries on that same transaction?

Yes—SET LOCAL affects the whole transaction on that connection. So if you run other queries in the same transaction, they’ll also use force_custom_plan. If you want to limit blast radius: - Use a dedicated short transaction that only runs this report query. - Or do it on a dedicated connection acquired from the pool: - Acquire conn - Begin - SET LOCAL plan_cache_mode = force_custom_plan - Run report - Commit - Release conn That keeps the setting from leaking to unrelated work (since SET LOCAL resets at transaction end). One nuance: force_custom_plan only matters for prepared statements. If your query is not being prepared/cached at the server, it won’t change much. Still safe.
For 365 days, the filtered_orders set is huge and query is slower (~6–7s). Any alternative plan for large windows? Maybe switch to a different strategy when selectivity is low?

Yes—when the time window is large, you’re close to “scan most of the table anyway”. The best plan can change: - For small windows (7/30/90 days): start from filtered orders, join to items/refunds, aggregate. - For large windows (365 days): it might be cheaper to aggregate order_items per order_id without filtering first (or to use a different join order), because the “filter” doesn’t cut much. But you don’t want the planner flipping unpredictably. Two approaches: A) Two explicit query variants in the app - If days <= 90: use the filtered_orders-driven query you have. - If days > 90 (or some threshold you measure): use an alternative that’s better for bulk. What “bulk” query could look like depends on data distribution, but a common pattern is: 1) Pre-aggregate items per order_id over the whole table (or over a broad range if you can restrict by order_id somehow—usually you can’t). 2) Join that to orders filtered by created_at, then to customers. However, note: pre-aggregating all order_items for every run can be very expensive too unless it can reuse cached data and parallelize well. B) Use a rolling summary table (even minimal) You said “no drastic schema changes”, but this is often the least invasive “big win” for 365-day queries: - Create a table like order_financials(order_id PK, gross numeric, refunded numeric). - Maintain it incrementally: - gross updated on item insert/update (or batch job). - refunded updated on refund insert/update. Then the report becomes: - Filter orders by created_at/status - Join to order_financials by order_id - Group by customer This turns the heavy 9.4M-row aggregation into a much smaller join/aggregate. If you truly can’t add maintenance logic right now, then the “two query variants” approach is the next best. Also check if 365-day query is mostly I/O: - Look at BUFFERS: are shared read blocks huge? If yes, it’s scanning lots from disk; caching or faster storage helps more than SQL tweaks. - If it’s CPU-bound, parallelism and avoiding spill help. If you paste EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) for the 365-day case, we can decide which alternative shape is likely better.
Suppose we add order_financials. How would you keep it correct without triggers? We can run a job every few minutes.

If you can tolerate a few minutes of staleness, a periodic job is usually simpler and safer than triggers under high write volume. A workable incremental approach: 1) Create the summary table - order_financials(order_id bigint primary key, gross numeric(12,2) not null, refunded numeric(12,2) not null, updated_at timestamptz not null) 2) Track “dirty” orders since last run You need a way to find orders whose items/refunds changed. Options: - If order_items and refunds have created_at / updated_at columns: use those. - If they don’t, you can still do it by looking at “recently inserted IDs” only, but updates/deletes become harder. - If you can add one column without “drastic schema change”, adding updated_at to order_items/refunds is extremely helpful. Assuming you have timestamps (best case): - In each job run, compute cutoff = now() - interval '5 minutes' (plus maybe overlap like 10 minutes for safety). - Find distinct order_ids from order_items where updated_at >= cutoff - Union distinct order_ids from refunds where updated_at >= cutoff - Recompute gross/refunded for just those order_ids and upsert into order_financials. Pseudo-SQL for the job: WITH dirty_orders AS ( SELECT DISTINCT order_id FROM order_items WHERE updated_at >= $cutoff UNION SELECT DISTINCT order_id FROM refunds WHERE updated_at >= $cutoff ), items AS ( SELECT oi.order_id, SUM(oi.qty*oi.price) AS gross FROM order_items oi JOIN dirty_orders d ON d.order_id = oi.order_id GROUP BY oi.order_id ), refs AS ( SELECT r.order_id, SUM(r.amount) AS refunded FROM refunds r JOIN dirty_orders d ON d.order_id = r.order_id GROUP BY r.order_id ) INSERT INTO order_financials(order_id, gross, refunded, updated_at) SELECT d.order_id, COALESCE(i.gross, 0), COALESCE(r.refunded, 0), now() FROM dirty_orders d LEFT JOIN items i ON i.order_id = d.order_id LEFT JOIN refs r ON r.order_id = d.order_id ON CONFLICT (order_id) DO UPDATE SET gross = EXCLUDED.gross, refunded = EXCLUDED.refunded, updated_at = EXCLUDED.updated_at; 3) Handle deletes / corrections - The above recompute-per-order handles updates and deletes fine as long as updated_at changes when rows change. - If you can’t guarantee updated_at, you need another mechanism: an explicit “dirty_orders” queue table populated by app on writes (often easier than triggers). 4) Indexing for the job - order_items(order_id) INCLUDE (qty, price) already helps. - If using updated_at: index order_items(updated_at, order_id) or (updated_at) INCLUDE (order_id). Same for refunds. Then the report query becomes much lighter: WITH filtered_orders AS (...) SELECT c.id, COUNT(*) AS order_count, SUM(of.gross) AS gross, SUM(of.refunded) AS refunded FROM filtered_orders fo JOIN customers c ON c.id = fo.customer_id JOIN order_financials of ON of.order_id = fo.id WHERE c.status='active' GROUP BY c.id ORDER BY gross DESC LIMIT 50; If you tell me whether order_items/refunds currently have any timestamp columns, I can tailor the incremental strategy.
order_items has created_at but not updated_at; refunds has created_at. Updates are rare but possible. Deletes basically never. Could we approximate with created_at only?

Yes, with your constraints you can get “mostly correct” with created_at-only, and you can make it fully correct if you add a periodic full reconciliation. Two-tier approach: 1) Frequent incremental job (every few minutes) using created_at - Dirty orders are those with new items/refunds since last watermark. - This will miss updates to old rows (rare) but will catch new activity. Keep a watermark table: - job_state(name text primary key, last_created_at timestamptz) Each run: - cutoff = last_created_at - overlap (e.g., 5 minutes) to avoid missing late-arriving transactions - dirty_orders from order_items where created_at >= cutoff union refunds where created_at >= cutoff - Recompute totals for those orders and upsert. Then advance last_created_at to now(). 2) Daily (or hourly) reconciliation job for correctness Because updates are rare, you can do a slower job off-peak: - Find orders updated in the last day/week by some proxy: - If orders has updated_at, use that. - If not, just recompute all orders in last N days (e.g., last 7 days) nightly. That bounds the cost and catches most corrections. - Or, if volume allows, recompute everything nightly (probably too expensive with 9.4M items, but maybe acceptable off-peak with parallelism). Given you already run reports for windows up to 365 days, I’d suggest: - Incremental job: continuous, based on created_at. - Reconcile last 30 days nightly (or last 90 days weekly), depending on how far back updates can occur. If updates can happen arbitrarily far back, then you either accept eventual inconsistency for old orders, or you need a real “dirty tracking” mechanism (app-side queue table is the usual minimal-intrusion way).
If we do order_financials, do we still need the INCLUDE indexes on order_items/refunds?

For the report query itself, no—the report would no longer read order_items/refunds. But you likely still want good indexes for the job that maintains order_financials: - For recomputing a set of dirty order_ids: - order_items(order_id) INCLUDE (qty, price) is still excellent (lets you sum without hitting heap much, when vacuumed). - refunds(order_id) INCLUDE (amount) likewise. - For finding dirty order_ids by created_at cutoff: - order_items(created_at) INCLUDE (order_id) or (created_at, order_id) - refunds(created_at) INCLUDE (order_id) or (created_at, order_id) Which is better depends on how you query: - If you do WHERE created_at >= cutoff then select distinct order_id, an index on (created_at, order_id) is a good fit. - If you already have order_items(order_id) and created_at is only used for dirty detection, you might add the created_at index specifically for that. So you might end up with two indexes per table. If that’s too much write overhead, you can compromise: - Keep (created_at, order_id) and drop the INCLUDE index, and accept some heap reads during recompute; or - Keep (order_id) INCLUDE (...) and accept a seq scan over “recent” created_at if recent volume is small (often not true).
Got it. Back to the original live query: is there any benefit to changing numeric(12,2) arithmetic to integer cents for performance?

Usually not worth it just for this query unless you’re CPU-bound on the multiplication/sum and doing it at very high volume. numeric is slower than integer/float because it’s arbitrary precision. But in your current runtime profile, the big costs were I/O, joins, and aggregation memory/spill; once those are fixed, arithmetic can matter, but typically it’s a smaller fraction. If you’re curious, you can test: - Replace SUM(qty*price) with SUM((qty * (price*100)::bigint)) style (but that still uses numeric unless price stored as numeric). To really benefit, you’d need storage change (price in integer cents) which is a bigger schema change and has correctness implications (rounding rules, currency). If you implement order_financials, you can also precompute gross as numeric once per order and avoid repeated numeric ops in the report.
Makes sense. Any last Postgres settings worth considering for this kind of analytic query?

A few settings that often matter, but should be changed carefully and tested: 1) random_page_cost / effective_cache_size - If on SSD and lots of indexed access: random_page_cost ~ 1.1–1.5 is common. - effective_cache_size should reflect OS cache + shared_buffers (often 50–75% of RAM) to help planner choose index scans. 2) work_mem - Don’t crank globally. Prefer per-role or SET LOCAL for the report workload, as you’re already doing. 3) Parallelism - max_parallel_workers_per_gather: allow 2–8 depending on CPU and concurrency. - max_parallel_workers: overall cap. - But too much parallelism can hurt OLTP latency, so consider setting higher only for a reporting role/session. 4) Autovacuum tuning on write-heavy tables - Especially if you rely on index-only scans. - Lower scale factors on order_items; ensure autovacuum has enough workers/work_mem. 5) Statistics - Raise stats target on columns with skew (orders.created_at, orders.status). - Extended stats for correlated predicates (status + created_at). Beyond that, most further gains come from architectural changes (summary tables/materialized views/partitioning), not knobs.
Perfect. I’ll proceed with the per-query work_mem and consider order_financials.
Can you walk me through why Dijkstra's is O((V+E) log V) with a priority queue?

Sure. Let’s assume we’re using an adjacency list representation and a binary heap priority queue (min-heap). The high-level idea is: Dijkstra’s processes vertices in order of increasing tentative distance, repeatedly extracting the next closest vertex from the heap, and relaxing its outgoing edges. We can decompose the runtime into a few parts: 1) Initialization - We set dist[v] = ∞ for all v, dist[source] = 0. - We push the source into the heap (or push all vertices with their initial distances; implementations vary). This is O(V) for initializing the array, plus O(log V) for pushing the source (or O(V) heapify if pushing all at once). 2) Extract-min operations - Each time we take the next vertex u with the smallest tentative distance, we perform an extract-min from the heap. - In the typical “lazy” implementation (push new keys instead of decrease-key), you might extract more than V times due to duplicates. But for complexity analysis, we often discuss the “decrease-key capable” version or bound the lazy version separately. - In the classic analysis with decrease-key: each vertex is extracted once when it becomes final. So extract-min happens V times; each extract-min on a binary heap is O(log V), giving O(V log V). 3) Edge relaxations + decrease-key operations - For each edge (u, v), when u is extracted, we consider relaxing it: if dist[u] + w(u,v) < dist[v], we update dist[v]. - In a heap with decrease-key, an improvement triggers a decrease-key on v, which is O(log V). - Each edge is examined once when its source vertex is extracted (directed graph), so we have E relaxations. Not every relaxation causes a decrease-key, but at most one successful decrease-key per relaxation attempt, so O(E log V) in the worst case. Total: O(V log V + E log V) = O((V + E) log V). The adjacency list scanning contributes O(E) itself, but it’s dominated by the heap operations in this bound, unless you use a heap with faster decrease-key.
I’ve seen some sources say O(E log V) and others O((V+E) log V). Which is “more correct”?

Both are correct, but they’re emphasizing different things and making slightly different assumptions. - O((V+E) log V) is the clean decomposition: V extract-min operations plus up to E decrease-key operations. - O(E log V) often appears because, for connected graphs with E ≥ V - 1, E dominates V asymptotically, so (V+E) log V = O(E log V). It’s common to simplify to O(E log V) under the assumption the graph is connected and not extremely sparse. However, if the graph is very sparse and possibly disconnected (or you’re running Dijkstra from a source in a graph with many isolated vertices), then V log V might not be dominated by E log V. For instance, if E = O(V), they’re the same order; if E is much smaller than V (possible if there are many isolated vertices), then V log V matters. So “more correct” in a strict accounting sense is O((V+E) log V). “More common simplified” is O(E log V) assuming E is at least on the order of V.
You mentioned a “lazy” implementation without decrease-key. How does that affect the complexity?

Good question. In many languages/libraries, a standard priority queue doesn’t support decrease-key efficiently. A common workaround is: - When you find a better distance to v, instead of decreasing v’s key in the heap, you push a new entry (dist_new, v). - When you pop from the heap, you check if the popped distance matches the current best dist[v]. If it’s stale (greater than dist[v]), you skip it. Complexity implications: - You can push up to O(E) entries (in the worst case, every relaxation improves something, leading to a push). - You can also pop up to O(E) entries, because each push can lead to a future pop (even if skipped). Each push/pop is O(log M) where M is the heap size; M can grow to O(E). So you get O(E log E) in the worst case. Since log E and log V are within a constant factor when E is polynomial in V (e.g., E ≤ V^2), people often rewrite O(E log E) as O(E log V). More precisely: - If E ≤ V^2, then log E ≤ 2 log V, so O(E log E) = O(E log V). So in dense graphs, they’re equivalent up to a constant factor in the log. But in strict terms: - With lazy insertion: O(E log E) time, O(E) heap space. - With decrease-key: O((V+E) log V) time, O(V) heap space (plus adjacency).
Can you show the operation counts more explicitly? Like “this many pushes, this many pops”?

Yes. Let’s compare two variants. Variant A: Binary heap with decrease-key (conceptual) Assumptions: - Each vertex is in the heap with a key equal to its current tentative distance. - You can decrease-key in O(log V). Counts: - Build heap: either insert V items O(V log V) or heapify O(V). Many analyses assume heapify O(V). - extract-min: exactly V times (each vertex becomes “final” once) => V pops. - decrease-key: at most once per successful relaxation. Each edge (u,v) is examined once when u is extracted, so at most E relaxations, hence ≤ E decrease-keys. So: - Pops: V - Decrease-keys: ≤ E - Total heap operations: V extract-min + ≤ E decrease-key (+ build) - Each is O(log V) => O((V + E) log V). Variant B: Lazy binary heap without decrease-key Assumptions: - dist[v] stored in array. - Heap stores pairs (candidateDistance, v). We may store multiple candidates per vertex. Counts: - Push: each time we find a better distance to v, we push a new pair. How many improvements can happen? In worst case, each edge relaxation could improve something, so O(E) pushes. - Pop: In the worst case, you might pop all pushed entries. That’s O(E) pops. - Each push/pop is O(log H) where H ≤ number of entries in heap ≤ O(E). So: - Pushes: O(E) - Pops: O(E) - Each O(log E) => O(E log E). In practice, it’s often fine and simpler to implement.
I want to be really precise about why extract-min happens V times. What if some vertices are unreachable?

If some vertices are unreachable from the source, then in a typical implementation: - Their dist stays ∞. - Depending on how you initialize the heap, you might or might not ever extract them. Two common approaches: 1) Heap starts with only the source - You push (0, source). - You only push neighbors when discovered/improved. If a vertex is unreachable, it never gets pushed, and thus never extracted. Then the number of extract-min operations is equal to the number of reachable vertices R, not V. Complexity becomes O((R + E_reachable) log R) or similar, depending on how you count. 2) Heap starts with all vertices - You put all vertices in the heap with initial key dist[v] (∞ for most), then decrease-key as you find better distances. Then you do extract-min V times, because you will eventually pop even the ∞ ones at the end (unless you early-stop when min is ∞). Many implementations do early-stop: if the smallest key is ∞, you can break, since remaining vertices are unreachable. That reduces pops to R again. So the “V extract-min” statement is for the standard theoretical version where each vertex is extracted at most once and you assume you process all vertices (or you upper bound by V). Unreachable vertices don’t break the bound; they just reduce the actual number of operations.
Can we go through a tiny example and track the heap operations? I think that would cement it.

Let’s do a small directed weighted graph with nonnegative weights: Vertices: A, B, C, D Edges: A->B (1) A->C (4) B->C (2) B->D (6) C->D (3) Run Dijkstra from A. We’ll do the lazy heap version (push instead of decrease-key), since it’s easier to show without needing a special heap structure. Initialize: dist[A]=0, dist[B]=∞, dist[C]=∞, dist[D]=∞ heap = [(0,A)] Step 1: pop (0,A) - It’s current (0 == dist[A]), process A Relax A->B: dist[B] becomes 1, push (1,B) Relax A->C: dist[C] becomes 4, push (4,C) heap: [(1,B), (4,C)] Step 2: pop (1,B) - Current (1 == dist[B]) Relax B->C: candidate = 1+2=3 < dist[C]=4, update dist[C]=3, push (3,C) Relax B->D: candidate = 1+6=7 < dist[D]=∞, update dist[D]=7, push (7,D) heap: [(3,C), (4,C), (7,D)] (heap order abstract) Step 3: pop (3,C) - Current (3 == dist[C]) Relax C->D: candidate = 3+3=6 < dist[D]=7, update dist[D]=6, push (6,D) heap: [(4,C), (6,D), (7,D)] Step 4: pop (4,C) - Stale, because dist[C]=3 now; skip processing heap: [(6,D), (7,D)] Step 5: pop (6,D) - Current (6 == dist[D]) D has no outgoing edges in this example heap: [(7,D)] Step 6: pop (7,D) - Stale (dist[D]=6); skip heap empty, done. Counts: Pushes: (0,A), (1,B), (4,C), (3,C), (7,D), (6,D) => 6 pushes Pops: 6 pops Relaxations examined: outdegree(A)+outdegree(B)+outdegree(C)+outdegree(D when popped current) = 2+2+1+0 = 5 edges (E=5) Stale pops occurred for C and D duplicates. This shows how duplicates can appear; in worst-case graphs, duplicates can be O(E).
In the decrease-key version, would there be no duplicates?

Right. If you have a heap that supports decrease-key and you keep exactly one heap entry per vertex, then: - Each vertex appears once. - When you find a better distance, you decrease its key instead of pushing a new entry. So no duplicates, and extract-min happens at most once per vertex. The tradeoff is implementation complexity: you need a way to locate the heap position for a vertex in O(1) (like an index array) to perform decrease-key efficiently.
How does the analysis change if I use a Fibonacci heap?

With a Fibonacci heap, the amortized costs are: - insert: O(1) amortized - decrease-key: O(1) amortized - extract-min: O(log V) amortized In Dijkstra with a Fibonacci heap: - You still do V extract-min operations => O(V log V) - You do up to E decrease-key operations => O(E) - Inserts are O(V) or O(1) per inserted vertex, depending on initialization. Total: O(E + V log V), which is asymptotically better than O((V+E) log V) when E is much larger than V (dense graphs). But Fibonacci heaps have large constant factors and are complex, so in practice binary heaps are often faster.
People also mention pairing heaps. What’s the usual bound there?

Pairing heaps are simpler than Fibonacci heaps and often perform well in practice. Theoretical bounds are trickier: - insert: O(1) amortized - decrease-key: not as clean as Fibonacci; known bounds exist but historically were less tight; commonly cited as O(log V) amortized or “good in practice” - extract-min: O(log V) amortized For Dijkstra, many references treat pairing heaps similarly to Fibonacci heaps in practice, but the safe theoretical bound you’ll often see is still O((V+E) log V) unless you assume decrease-key is amortized O(1), which is not generally guaranteed as strongly as Fibonacci heaps. So: for interview-level discussion, it’s usually enough to know: - Binary heap: O((V+E) log V) - Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V)
What about adjacency matrix vs adjacency list? Does that change Dijkstra’s complexity?

Yes, significantly. Adjacency list + heap: - You scan outgoing edges of each extracted vertex, total scanning cost O(E). - Heap operations drive the log factor. Total: O((V+E) log V) with binary heap. Adjacency matrix + simple array (no heap): There’s a classic O(V^2) implementation: - Maintain dist[] and visited[]. - Repeat V times: find the unvisited vertex with smallest dist via a linear scan O(V); then relax all outgoing edges by scanning the entire row of the matrix O(V). Each iteration is O(V + V) = O(V), repeated V times => O(V^2). Adjacency matrix + heap: You can still use a heap, but relaxing edges from a vertex requires scanning all possible neighbors in that row, which is O(V) per extracted vertex, so O(V^2) scanning anyway. So the matrix tends to push you toward O(V^2) regardless. So: - Sparse graphs: adjacency list + heap is better. - Dense graphs: adjacency matrix O(V^2) might be competitive, since E ~ V^2 and O((V+E) log V) becomes O(V^2 log V), which is worse asymptotically than O(V^2).
Wait, for dense graphs, does the binary-heap Dijkstra become O(V^2 log V)? That seems worse than O(V^2). So is the matrix version actually asymptotically better?

Asymptotically, yes. If E is Θ(V^2), then: - Binary heap adjacency list: O((V + V^2) log V) = O(V^2 log V) - Matrix + linear scan: O(V^2) So for very dense graphs, the O(V^2) implementation is asymptotically better. But practical performance depends on constants, memory locality, and actual density. Adjacency matrices use O(V^2) space, which can be prohibitive. For moderate sizes and density, a matrix might be fast due to contiguous memory and simple operations, but it’s not always feasible.
How does this compare to BFS complexity? Like unweighted shortest paths.

For unweighted graphs (or graphs where all edges have the same weight), BFS gives shortest path distances in: - O(V + E) time with adjacency lists because: - Each vertex is enqueued/dequeued at most once. - Each edge is inspected once (directed) or twice (undirected). No heap, no log factor. Dijkstra is essentially BFS generalized to nonnegative weights, where the queue becomes a priority queue because the “frontier” is ordered by distance.
Could you explain the role of the “nonnegative weights” assumption in Dijkstra?

Dijkstra relies on a key invariant: when you extract the vertex u with the smallest tentative distance from the priority queue, dist[u] is final—it’s the true shortest distance from the source to u. Why does that hold? Because if all edge weights are nonnegative, any alternative path to u that goes through some other not-yet-extracted vertex x would have distance at least dist[x] (since x has tentative distance ≥ dist[u] when u is extracted) plus a nonnegative edge/path from x to u, making it ≥ dist[u]. So you can’t later discover a shorter path to u. If negative edges exist, you can extract u thinking it’s final, but later a path using a negative edge could produce a shorter distance, violating that invariant. That’s why Bellman–Ford (or Johnson’s algorithm) is used for graphs with negative weights (but no negative cycles for shortest paths to be well-defined).
Switching back to complexity: can you help me compare Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford vs Floyd-Warshall in Big-O terms?

Sure, focusing on standard implementations: 1) Dijkstra (single-source, nonnegative weights) - With binary heap + adjacency list: O((V+E) log V) - With Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V) - With adjacency matrix + linear scan: O(V^2) 2) Bellman–Ford (single-source, can handle negative weights, detects negative cycles) - Standard: O(VE) Because it relaxes all edges V-1 times, plus optionally one more pass to detect negative cycles. 3) Floyd–Warshall (all-pairs shortest paths, can handle negative weights, but not negative cycles) - O(V^3) time, O(V^2) space It’s dynamic programming over intermediate vertices. Rule-of-thumb: - Single-source on sparse graph with nonnegative weights: Dijkstra. - Single-source with negative edges: Bellman–Ford (or reweighting + Dijkstra). - All-pairs with dense-ish graph and moderate V: Floyd–Warshall. - All-pairs on sparse graph: run Dijkstra from each vertex (O(VE log V)) or Johnson’s algorithm.
For interview purposes, when asked “What is the complexity of Dijkstra?”, what answer should I give?

A good interview answer is: - “With an adjacency list and a binary heap priority queue, Dijkstra runs in O((V+E) log V).” Optionally add: - “If you implement it with an array / adjacency matrix, it’s O(V^2).” - “With a Fibonacci heap, it can be O(E + V log V) amortized.” Then you can ask/clarify what data structures are assumed.
I’m still a bit fuzzy on why decrease-key is O(log V) in a binary heap.

In a binary heap, the heap property is: each node’s key is ≤ its children’s keys (for a min-heap). The heap is stored in an array where parent/child indices are easy to compute. Decrease-key operation: - You locate the element whose key decreases (you need its index in the array). - You set its key to the smaller value. - That might violate the heap property with its parent (because the key got smaller). - So you “bubble up” (sift up): while the element’s key is smaller than its parent’s key, swap it with its parent. In the worst case, the element moves from a leaf to the root, which is the height of the heap. A binary heap with V elements has height O(log V). Each swap is O(1), so total is O(log V). Extract-min similarly does: - Remove root (min). - Move last element to root. - “Bubble down” (sift down) to restore heap property, again O(log V).
If I don’t have an index to locate the element, decrease-key becomes expensive, right?

Exactly. If you don’t know where the vertex’s heap entry is, you can’t do decrease-key efficiently because you’d have to search the heap array to find it, which is O(V). That ruins the runtime. That’s why decrease-key implementations often maintain: - heap array of vertex IDs - pos[vertex] = index in heap array So you can find an element in O(1) and then bubble it up in O(log V). In many standard library priority queues, you can’t access internal indices, so you can’t implement decrease-key directly; hence the lazy duplicate-push workaround.
